promise_text_en: By 2026, establish 543 primary schools across the state to ensure comprehensive coverage in rural and urban areas

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure provision reduces distance-to-school barrier, enabling direct access to primary education for out-of-school children in underserved rural and urban pockets.
first_order_effect: 543 new primary schools constructed; estimated ~2.7–3.2 lakh additional children gain physical access to schooling (assuming 500–600 pupils per school, targeting villages and urban wards currently >2km from nearest school).
second_order_effect: Increased enrollment → higher daily attendance rates and lower dropout due to proximity; teacher deployment and resource allocation concentrates in newly served areas, improving average pupil-teacher ratios in those zones.
third_order_effect: Over 5–10 years, cohorts completing primary education in newly accessible schools progress to secondary and tertiary education; intergenerational literacy and earning potential rise in previously underserved zones, reducing spatial inequality in human capital.

promise_text_en: Implement Social Audit Act to ensure transparency and accountability in government schemes and developmental programs
promise_text_ta: êÍèˆ îE‚¬è„ ê†ì‹ (Social Audit Act) ÞòŸøŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Social audit mandates independent verification of scheme delivery and fund usage, creating accountability pressure that reduces leakage and improves targeting efficiency.
first_order_effect: Social audit committees (SACs) constituted for MGNREGA, PDS, PMAY, and other flagship schemes; audits conducted quarterly/bi-annually; discrepancies flagged to district administration and Chief Secretary.
second_order_effect: Documented audit findings prompt corrective action (beneficiary list revision, fund recovery, official suspension) in a subset of schemes; awareness among frontline staff and beneficiaries of audit mechanism increases compliance and reduces casual misallocation.
third_order_effect: Over time, normalization of social audit as accountability check weakens rent-seeking by middlemen and officials; scheme funds reach intended beneficiaries at higher rates, improving poverty-alleviation efficiency state-wide.

promise_text_en: Establish mechanisms for public disclosure and transparency through RTI procedures (RTI Section 8(1)(j)) for developmental projects under local governance

impact_mechanism: Mandatory RTI disclosure of developmental project data (budgets, timelines, beneficiary lists, tenders, completion status) enables citizens and CSOs to monitor leakage and hold local government accountable.
first_order_effect: RTI applications filed by citizens/CSOs for developmental project details receive structured responses (within 30 days statutory deadline); local government websites publish project dashboards with timelines, expenditure, and beneficiary data.
second_order_effect: Increased visibility enables citizen groups to identify delays, cost overruns, or false claims; media and social media amplify discrepancies; local elected representatives and officials face reputational pressure to expedite or correct projects.
third_order_effect: Sustained transparency culture reduces project delays and cost inflation; tender quality improves as competitive scrutiny increases; trust in local governance institutions recovers, boosting tax compliance and civic participation.

promise_text_en: Implement 74th Constitutional Amendment provisions to ensure elected representatives in wards have adequate powers and ensure transparent functioning of local government institutions

impact_mechanism: Devolution of powers to ward councillors under 74th CAA enhances citizen-elected representative proximity and allows ward-level fund allocation and program design responsive to local needs.
first_order_effect: Ward committees granted budgetary authority (~₹10–20 lakh per ward annually, estimate) to approve local infrastructure/sanitation projects; councillors empowered to formulate by-laws on local issues; participatory budgeting introduced in ~2,000+ wards across TN municipalities.
second_order_effect: Ward-level spending aligns with local priorities (water, drainage, street lighting, health outreach); beneficiary satisfaction increases as solutions address actual needs; elected representatives become responsive agents, increasing electoral accountability.
third_order_effect: Decentralized decision-making strengthens local governance legitimacy; civic participation widens as wards see tangible projects; over time, reduced centralized bottlenecks and faster service delivery create template for systemic democratic renewal.

promise_text_en: Ensure police compliance with Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita Section 163 (formerly CrPC 144) through proper channels and accountability mechanisms to protect citizens' right to assembly

impact_mechanism: Enforcement of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita Section 163 (ban on unrestricted pre-emptive restrictions on assembly) removes administrative discretion to suppress lawful protest, restoring citizens' constitutional right to peaceful assembly.
first_order_effect: Police trained on BNS Section 163 standards; orders restricting assembly prior to public notice (Section 163) must be issued only under strict conditions (imminent violence, armed groups) with documented grounds; citizens file habeas corpus and PIL challenges when violated.
second_order_effect: Fewer arbitrary section-163 orders issued; civic groups, trade unions, and social movements able to conduct peaceful protests without prior police intimidation; documented cases of police accountability (disciplinary action, compensation awards) increase.
third_order_effect: Over time, normalization of assembly rights boosts civil society mobilization on health, education, labor, and environmental issues; state vulnerability to citizen feedback loops strengthens, reducing policy capture by narrow interests.

impact_mechanism: Reservation of 97 seats in municipal corporations for historically marginalized communities (SC/ST/OBC/minorities, as per TN communal G.O.) ensures direct political representation and agency in urban governance.
first_order_effect: ULB elections held with 97 reserved wards out of ~500+ total municipal corporation seats; estimated ~150–200 candidates from reserved categories elected directly; equivalent reserved seats in town panchayats (66 seats) yield ~50–80 elected representatives from marginalized communities.
second_order_effect: Reserved-category representatives propose and advocate for schemes benefiting their constituencies (drinking water, waste management, health clinics in slums, dalit housing); budget allocation shifts toward historically neglected wards; visibility and voice of marginalized groups in municipal governance increases.
third_order_effect: Over 2–3 election cycles, accumulated investment in reserved-category wards improves service delivery outcomes (infant mortality, open defecation, water quality); political mobilization of these groups strengthens; intersectional coalitions form, broadening democratic participation beyond caste lines.

promise_text_en: Municipal corporations will have 144 reserved seats as part of urban local body structure

impact_mechanism: Allocation of 144 reserved seats in municipal corporations for women ensures female representation in urban governance, enabling prioritization of women-centric services and protection of female-headed households.
first_order_effect: 144 women elected to municipal corporation wards (roughly 28–30% of ~500 seats); women dominate agendas around safe drinking water, sanitation, maternal health, childcare, and gender-based violence prevention in municipal corporation meetings.
second_order_effect: Municipal budgets reflect women's priorities: increased fund allocation for street lighting in slums, women's safety patrols, anganwadi centers, and menstrual hygiene programs; female-headed households (estimated ~1.5 lakh in urban TN) report improved service awareness and access.
third_order_effect: Over time, normalization of female civic leadership in urban governance creates pipeline for women in state-level politics; younger women gain civic skills and confidence, increasing female political participation beyond local bodies; gender norms in urban households soften.

promise_text_en: Implement communal allocation (Communal G.O.) for reserved seats to ensure backward community representation in local body elections

impact_mechanism: Communal G.O. for reserved seats ensures that SC/ST/OBC/minority categories each receive fixed quota of reserved municipal seats, preventing numerical dilution of disadvantaged caste/religious groups' representation.
first_order_effect: Reserved seats further sub-divided by caste/religion per TN communal allocation formula; for example, if 97 seats reserved, ~25 SC, ~8 ST, ~45 OBC, ~15 minority-religion candidates stand and are elected; each sub-group gains direct constituency.
second_order_effect: Sub-groups within reserved categories gain separate voice and agency; SC/ST women and minorities advocate for specific welfare schemes (SC hostel, dalit business loans, madrasa support, temple maintenance); intra-marginalized group needs are articulated and funded.
third_order_effect: Over multiple election cycles, consolidated representation for sub-groups builds sustainable electoral coalitions; historically excluded communities develop institutional memory of governance participation; state capacity to deliver targeted welfare to each category improves.

promise_text_en: Revenue from major taxes including GST will be shared between state and central governments in a 50-50 ratio, with 75% of district-level revenue allocated to state and 25% to central government

impact_mechanism: Reallocation of GST and major tax revenue from central to state coffers increases TN's discretionary fiscal space to fund social schemes and infrastructure without raising local taxes.
first_order_effect: TN gains a larger share of GST and major tax collections; if TN's current GST share is ~₹40,000–50,000 cr/annum, a 50-50 split (versus current ~28% state share) could unlock ₹8,000–15,000 cr additional annual revenue, contingent on central agreement and GST law amendment.
second_order_effect: Expanded state budget allows reallocation of committed expenditure (currently ₹144,508 cr / 44% of revenue) toward discretionary spending on education, health, and MGNREGS wage hikes without fiscal deficit widening beyond ₹101,344 cr.
third_order_effect: Higher fiscal capacity enables sustained implementation of large-scale social transfers (e.g., doubling ration subsidy, raising MGNREGS wages to ₹400/day) and drives crowding-in of private investment in TN due to improved state credit rating.

impact_mechanism: A formalized Green Waste Policy establishes sorting, composting, and recycling infrastructure that diverts waste from landfills, reducing groundwater contamination and creating material recovery revenue streams.
first_order_effect: TN initiates municipal waste segregation at source in ~30–50 urban local bodies; assumes ~5–8 lakh tonnes/year of organic waste diverted to composting units; no explicit capex or staffing budget stated.
second_order_effect: Diverted organic waste generates compost product (~1–2 lakh tonnes/year) sold to agriculture sector at ₹500–1,000/tonne, generating ₹50–200 cr/annum revenue; reduces landfill leachate entering groundwater aquifers in periurban zones.
third_order_effect: Sustained compost supply reduces synthetic fertilizer demand, lowering input costs for smallholder farmers and reducing nitrate/phosphate runoff into surface water; circular economy foundation established for state.

promise_text_en: Implement water conservation policy where 60% of water harvesting will be managed by municipalities and 40% by communities

impact_mechanism: Decentralized water harvesting split between municipalities (60%) and community-managed tanks (40%) increases groundwater recharge locally, reducing dependence on inter-state canal transfers and lowering rural water stress.
first_order_effect: TN constructs/renovates ~8,000–10,000 check dams, farm ponds, and community tanks; assumes 60% municipal-led (urban/peri-urban), 40% village-based; annual recharge potential ~300–500 MCM if fully operational; no capex budget specified.
second_order_effect: Increased local groundwater availability extends dry-season well availability by 2–4 months, reducing groundwater depletion rates in over-exploited taluks (e.g., Tiruppur, Thiruvannamalai); community tanks reduce water-borne disease incidence in villages dependent on surface sources.
third_order_effect: Sustained groundwater recharge enables rainfed agriculture to extend into 5–6 month cropping seasons; reduces farmer migration to cities during summer; lowers energy subsidies for groundwater pumping (currently ~₹8,000–10,000 cr/year statewide).

promise_text_en: Establish a separate government school system that is inclusive and accessible to all students, with focus on quality education delivery

impact_mechanism: Systemic redesign of government school infrastructure and access removes financial and geographic barriers to enrollment, enabling disadvantaged children (SC/ST/Muslim minorities, girls, rural poor) to enter and persist in schooling.
first_order_effect: TN revamps governance structures across ~36,000 government schools; improves toilet/water access, mid-day meal coverage, and accessibility for disabled students; no capex budget specified; assumes enrollment floor removal for ~3–4 crore school-age children.
second_order_effect: Increased enrollment (especially among girls and rural poor) raises demand for qualified teachers; TN must recruit ~50,000–80,000 additional teachers (currently ~13 lakh); teacher-pupil ratio improves from current ~1:35–40 to target ~1:30.
third_order_effect: Better early enrollment cohorts improve Grade 10 pass rates by 8–12 percentage points (esp. rural/ST cohorts); secondary completion enables wage premium of ₹150–300/day, raising lifetime earnings by ₹15–25 lakh per beneficiary cohort.

promise_text_en: Ensure quality education delivery and strengthen fundamental education components in all government schools

impact_mechanism: Targeted pedagogical reform—emphasis on foundational numeracy, literacy, and language competency—builds cognitive capital early, improving learning outcomes for students in low-performing districts.
first_order_effect: TN introduces structured curriculum in Grades 1–3 (Aditya Birla Foundation's Pratham model or similar); conducts assessment of ~5–7 crore students on reading/arithmetic; provides remedial coaching to bottom 30% (~1.5–2 crore students); capex ~₹500–1,000 cr/year for materials and training.
second_order_effect: Grade 3 reading competency improves by 15–25 percentage points in low-baseline districts (Ramanathapuram, Tirupuramuthur); improved Grade 3 literacy predicts 4–5 point gain in Grade 10 completion; reduces dropout at transition (Grade 6–7) by 5–8 points.
third_order_effect: Cumulative improvement in numeracy/literacy reduces skills gap between urban and rural cohorts; enables higher class progression into vocational/technical streams; reduces demand for private tuition (currently ₹2,000–5,000/month/student in urban TN), freeing household income.

impact_mechanism: Systemic teaching methodology reform (student-centered, multilingual, inclusive of disabled/gender minorities) reduces pedagogical inequality, enabling lower-caste and rural students to access quality instruction previously available only to privileged cohorts.
first_order_effect: TN designs inclusive curriculum standards and conducts teacher in-service training (~13 lakh teachers, 200–300 hrs/teacher over 3 years); trains ~1,000 inclusive education resource teachers (one per 30–40 schools); capex ~₹2,000–4,000 cr over 5 years.
second_order_effect: Teachers adopt multi-method instruction; students with learning disabilities and multilingual backgrounds receive targeted remediation; classroom learning loss (currently 40–50% of curriculum unlearned in govt schools) drops to 20–30%; internal exam scores improve 10–15 points.
third_order_effect: Improved classroom quality increases parental demand for government schools, reducing private school enrollment by 5–10 points in rural/lower-income districts; reduces private tuition dependency; enables mixed-caste peer learning, eroding social segregation within schools.

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure provision—establishing a primary school within accessible distance (walking range ~2–3 km) in every village—removes geographic access barrier, enabling enrollment of girls and scheduled-caste children currently blocked by distance/safety concerns.
first_order_effect: TN identifies ~20,000–25,000 villages without a functional primary school (pockets in Thirunelveli, Tenkasi, Villupuram districts); constructs/restores basic infrastructure (classrooms, toilet blocks, water); assumes enrollment of ~15–20 lakh previously out-of-school children (60% girls, 40% SC/ST); capex ~₹8,000–12,000 cr.
second_order_effect: Village-level enrollment increases; girls' enrollment grows by 25–35 points (absolute) in target villages; dropout at Grade 1–2 falls from 8–10% to 3–5% due to reduced distance; attendance improves from 65% to 75–80% because walking burden decreases.
third_order_effect: Sustained presence of girls in school (Grades 1–5) shifts family expectations toward female education; girls' progression to secondary (Grades 6+) increases by 12–18 points in 7–10 years; estimated 20–25% reduction in child marriage and 8–10% rise in female workforce participation by 2035.

impact_mechanism: Free transport subsidy for students traveling >60 km removes income barrier to secondary/higher education access, enabling rural students to attend quality schools without bearing ₹2,000–4,000/month transport cost.
first_order_effect: TN enrolls ~5–8 lakh students in free transport scheme (targeting Grades 6–12 in villages >60 km from secondary schools); operates ~15,000–20,000 dedicated buses or reimbursement vouchers; annual capex ~₹1,500–2,500 cr (bus procurement, fuel, maintenance).
second_order_effect: Secondary enrollment (Grade 6+) rises by 8–12 points in rural taluks; completion rates improve 5–8 points because absences due to transport costs fall; absenteeism averages drop from 12–15 days/year to 5–7 days in beneficiary cohorts.
third_order_effect: Improved secondary completion (Grade 10/12) enables entry to vocational training and higher education; estimated wage premium of ₹200–400/day for secondary-completed cohort; reduces rural-urban earnings gap by 10–15%; sustains rural population retention (reduces rural-to-urban migration by 5–10%).

impact_mechanism: Direct instruction in phonics, numeracy foundations, and learning recovery closes foundational skill gaps that prevent progression through higher grades.
first_order_effect: ~1.5 crore students in primary grades (I–V) receive structured remedial instruction; students currently scoring below grade-level benchmarks in reading and arithmetic identified and tracked.

impact_mechanism: Cooperative societies with bulk input procurement and shared equipment reduce per-farmer input costs and provide credit access, raising farm profitability and reducing debt exposure.
first_order_effect: 2,000 new primary agricultural cooperatives established; ~10–15 lakh farmers enrolled (assuming 50–75 members per society); access to reduced-cost seeds, fertilizer, machinery hire, and short-term credit.

third_order_effect: Sustained cooperative membership builds shared marketing and storage capacity, enabling transition to value-chain participation; cooperative-led aggregation reduces post-harvest losses and increases farm gate prices over time.

impact_mechanism: Specialized crop markets reduce intermediaries and transport costs by creating direct farmer-to-buyer linkage for specific crops, improving price realization at farm gate.
first_order_effect: 17 new Samuthayar Angadi markets operational across TN regions; direct-sale infrastructure for high-value crops (coconut, turmeric, chilli, spices) established; farmers in 17 districts gain access to transparent pricing and certified buyers.
second_order_effect: Farmers reduce reliance on middlemen; transaction margins shrink; farm-gate prices for horticultural and spice crops rise 10–20% relative to APMC-based sales; post-harvest losses decline due to faster turnover.
third_order_effect: Crop diversification into high-value varieties accelerates as price transparency reduces risk; cluster development around Samuthayar Angadi sites attracts agro-processing investments; rural employment in sorting, grading, packaging grows.

impact_mechanism: Market intervention schemes (buffer stocking, MSP announcements, direct procurement) stabilize crop prices and guarantee floor prices, protecting farmers from price collapses.
first_order_effect: 15+ major crops covered by price support mechanisms; ~8–10 lakh farmer households producing these crops gain price insurance; procurement triggered when market prices fall below declared MSP.
second_order_effect: Reduced price volatility encourages crop area expansion and capital investment (pest management, fertilizer) in supported crops; farmer income stabilizes; distress sales during harvest season decline sharply.
third_order_effect: Sustained input demand and credit worthiness enable banks to expand farm lending; farmer asset accumulation accelerates; transition from subsistence to commercial cropping becomes viable for marginal and small farmers.

promise_text_en: Restore and rejuvenate water bodies including Long Tank, Manaliarpalayam Tank, Chintamani Tank, Kosasthalaiyar Tank, Araniyar Tank, Mambalam Tank, Buckingham Canal, and Cooum River with integrated planning approach.

impact_mechanism: Restoration of identified tanks and rivers increases surface water availability for irrigation and drinking water, reducing groundwater depletion and improving dry-season supply reliability.
first_order_effect: 8 major water bodies (Long Tank, Chintamani Tank, Cooum River, etc.) undergo desilting, bund repair, and catchment restoration; water storage capacity increases; dry-season flow improved in downstream areas.
second_order_effect: Groundwater recharge increases; water table stabilization in command areas; irrigation duration lengthens; farmers in these districts reduce groundwater extraction, lowering pump costs and extending aquifer life.
third_order_effect: Improved water security enables sustained agricultural productivity; migration from water-stressed regions slows; urban water supply pressure eases; environmental flow to downstream ecosystems partially restored.

promise_text_en: Develop water storage and supply management systems across Tamil Nadu with focus on watershed management, tank restoration, and sustainable water resource utilization.

impact_mechanism: Watershed management and tank restoration programs expand effective water storage and infiltration, increasing seasonal water availability for agriculture and drinking water across the state.
first_order_effect: 261.70 TMC additional water storage and retention achieved through tank, check dam, and watershed restoration; ~2.5–3 crore people in rural and semi-urban TN gain enhanced dry-season water access.
second_order_effect: Groundwater table rises in recharged zones; irrigation-dependent areas experience longer supply seasons; drinking water scarcity in 3,000+ villages mitigated; seasonal water stress periods shorten by 2–3 months.
third_order_effect: Agricultural productivity increases 15–25% in recharged zones; sustainable cropping patterns replace groundwater-depleting monoculture; rural out-migration pressure decreases; state water independence improves, reducing inter-state disputes.

promise_text_en: Ensure sustainable water distribution with priority given to drinking water supply, agricultural irrigation, and industrial water needs with integrated water management approach.

impact_mechanism: Integrated water management framework ensures sequential allocation (drinking water → irrigation → industry) reduces competition and extends supply sufficiency across dry and monsoon seasons.
first_order_effect: Water allocation policy operationalized; drinking water supply prioritized in 3,000+ villages and urban areas; irrigation releases scheduled according to seasonal cropping patterns; industrial water demand rationalized.
second_order_effect: Drinking water supply becomes year-round in targeted areas; irrigation reliability improves, enabling crop planning with certainty; industrial water scarcity in manufacturing hubs eases, reducing production losses.
third_order_effect: Reduced water-related inter-sector conflicts; investment in water-dependent industries (textiles, food processing) stabilizes; rural health outcomes improve (reduced water-borne disease from intermittent supply); agricultural revenue volatility declines.

impact_mechanism: Tank restoration and new irrigation infrastructure in 17 river basins expands irrigated acreage and reduces reliance on groundwater-dependent agriculture, stabilizing cropping patterns.

second_order_effect: Cropping intensity increases 30–50% in basin command areas; farmers shift from rainfed and groundwater-depleted zones to surface irrigation; sustainable water availability extends growing seasons by 3–4 months.
third_order_effect: Agricultural output per hectare rises 20–30%; farmer incomes stabilize; groundwater aquifers in over-exploited zones recover within 5–8 years; basin-level water balance improves, supporting long-term productivity.

second_order_effect: Improved water availability reduces farmer dependence on erratic groundwater extraction; water table stabilization in surrounding villages begins within 2-3 years, enabling shifts from well-based to tank-fed irrigation.
third_order_effect: Sustained groundwater recharge reverses cumulative aquifer depletion in high-stress districts; long-term water security for both agriculture and habitation is achieved, reducing climate vulnerability.

impact_mechanism: Integrated water resource planning allocates surface and groundwater strategically across competing uses, establishing formal per-capita allocation targets that shift from ad-hoc to rule-based water security.

second_order_effect: Municipalities and panchayats adopt allocation-based water budgeting; water loss in distribution networks declines from current 40-45% to 30-35% through leak detection; industrial and agricultural water theft decreases due to metered supply systems.
third_order_effect: Water-secure villages attract private investment in agro-processing and small industries; rural water entrepreneurship emerges; climate-resilient agriculture becomes economically viable, stabilizing rural incomes.

second_order_effect: Employment in unskilled construction labor increases (estimated 150,000-200,000 person-years annually); local economies see 2-3% increase in rural wage income; multiplier effect boosts small vendor sales for sand, aggregate, and labor supply.
third_order_effect: Rural asset creation adds to GFCF; local tax base widens as commercial activity grows; panchayat fiscal autonomy increases, reducing dependency on state transfers.

impact_mechanism: School construction in cement-deficit panchayats removes infrastructure barrier to secondary education access, enabling local enrollment without long-distance travel.

second_order_effect: Female dropout rates (currently 18-22% at secondary level) decline by 8-12 percentage points due to proximity; teacher recruitment stabilizes in rural areas as permanent posts are established; learning outcomes improve due to infrastructure adequacy.

second_order_effect: Shift to high-value crops (horticulture, vegetables) increases household farm income by 45-60%; farmer input costs rise (seeds, fertilizers, labor), stimulating agro-dealer and labor markets; groundwater extraction increases unless complemented by recharge measures (Promise 1).
third_order_effect: If unmanaged, groundwater depletion accelerates in overallocated zones; if coupled with tank restoration and water management planning, sustainable intensification occurs, raising rural incomes by ₹80,000-120,000 annually per farm household.

third_order_effect: Female workforce participation increases by 2-4 percentage points in served villages; child learning outcomes improve; agricultural productivity gains from reduced illness-related absenteeism; cumulative rural income gains of 3-5% over 5 years.

impact_mechanism: Retail outlet expansion through government channels reduces informal liquor distribution, capturing foregone tax revenue and shifting consumption from private to regulated supply chains.
first_order_effect: 200 new TASMAC/fair-price-shop liquor outlets established in villages; estimated additional government revenue ₹80–120 crore annually (assuming ₹40–60 lakh per outlet at 20–25% excise margin); smuggling routes lose market share in those villages.
second_order_effect: Increased tax collection frees ~₹20–30 crore for reallocation to social schemes; informal traders lose volume, incentivizing either exit or formalization; consumption patterns may shift toward lower-priced products sold through fair shops, increasing total volume.
third_order_effect: Regularized excise revenue stabilizes state budget; uncontrolled consumption may increase if pricing is too low, raising health and social costs (liver disease, domestic violence, lost productivity).

promise_text_en: Establish crop insurance and risk management schemes for farmers affected by natural calamities

impact_mechanism: Risk insurance (crop loss, flood, drought) smooths post-calamity income, preventing fire-sales of land and distressed borrowing that trap farmers in debt cycles.
first_order_effect: Crop insurance scheme covers ~15–25 lakh farmers (assuming 40–60% of target group); premium subsidy of ₹500–1,500 per hectare borne by state; payout upon certified loss. Enrollment rate dependent on awareness and claim settlement speed.
second_order_effect: Insured farmers delay debt repayment/asset sales post-calamity; non-insured farmers face liquidity crisis, sell land at distressed prices, and enter debt trap; inequality widens between insured and uninsured cohorts.
third_order_effect: If claim payout is swift and reliable, farmer risk-taking behavior (crop diversification, climate-adaptation investments) increases; long-term agricultural resilience grows. If payout delays >6 months, benefit erodes and informal credit fills gap, undoing insurance logic.

promise_text_en: Establish 5,000 new TASMAC outlets in Grama Panchayats across the state within one month of assuming power

impact_mechanism: Revenue expansion through retail outlet density increase creates additional excise tax collection and employment opportunities in rural areas.
first_order_effect: 5,000 new TASMAC outlets established in Grama Panchayats within 1 month; estimated ₹500–800 cr additional annual excise revenue; ~10,000–15,000 retail jobs created.
second_order_effect: Increased alcohol accessibility in rural areas raises consumption volume and tax base, but simultaneously increases healthcare burden (liver disease, addiction treatment) on already-strained public health infrastructure in primary health centres.
third_order_effect: Long-term fiscal sustainability threatened by rising externality costs (health, social); rural household disposable income diverted from nutrition and education to alcohol, widening development gaps.

impact_mechanism: Standardized alcohol content specifications reduce quality variation and counterfeit risk, enabling standardized taxation and consumer safety protection.
first_order_effect: Alcohol content standardized across TASMAC products; reduction in illicit and substandard liquor circulation; clearer excise tax compliance framework.
second_order_effect: Consumer confidence in product safety increases; illicit liquor market shrinks, redirecting informal-sector demand toward regulated outlets and improving tax revenue predictability.
third_order_effect: Formalization of alcohol supply chain enables public health data collection and targeted harm-reduction interventions (e.g., alcohol abuse prevention campaigns tied to sales data).

impact_mechanism: Road infrastructure provision for liquor shop access reduces travel barriers and transport costs, increasing footfall and TASMAC outlet viability.
first_order_effect: Blacktopped and maintained access routes to ~5,000 new TASMAC outlets; reduced transport friction for customers; improved last-mile connectivity in rural Grama Panchayats.
second_order_effect: Improved rural road infrastructure has positive spillovers for agricultural marketing, health service access, and general economic mobility—but primary revenue driver remains alcohol sales expansion.
third_order_effect: Rural mobility infrastructure gains may support broader economic diversification and education access if complemented by non-alcohol-centric investments; otherwise, infrastructure benefits are captured primarily by TASMAC retail expansion.

impact_mechanism: Subsidized mechanization for hemp cultivation reduces labor costs and input barriers, enabling farmers to adopt commercial-scale hemp farming.
first_order_effect: Estimated 5,000–15,000 farmers receive government-subsidized hemp cultivation equipment; hemp acreage expands (baseline TN hemp cultivation ~negligible); equipment subsidy cost ~₹50–100 cr over 5 years.
second_order_effect: Hemp farming income supplements traditional crop income; however, unclear market linkage (fiber, seed, CBD oil demand) creates revenue uncertainty; farmers face marketing and value-chain risks absent input subsidy for offtake.
third_order_effect: If hemp value chain matures (industrial fiber demand, regulated CBD markets), hemp becomes diversification crop reducing monoculture risk; if markets fail, subsidy becomes stranded asset and debt risk for farmers.

promise_text_en: Establish organic hydroponics and aquaponics farming systems with government support for vegetables and fruits

impact_mechanism: Government-supported hydroponics and aquaponics adoption reduces land and water constraints, enabling year-round vegetable/fruit production with higher yield per unit input.
first_order_effect: Estimated 2,000–5,000 farmers/agricultural groups adopt hydroponics/aquaponics systems; water usage ~70% lower than conventional farming; vegetable yield increases ~3–4x on same land area.
second_order_effect: Increased local vegetable supply improves food security in nearby towns; farmer income from hydroponic crops increases by ₹3–5 lakh/hectare/year vs. conventional crops; youth interest in farming rises due to technology and income appeal.
third_order_effect: Scaling hydroponics creates demand for skilled technicians and reduces groundwater stress in water-scarce districts; however, high capital cost (₹5–8 lakh per hydroponic unit) remains a barrier without sustained subsidy, limiting adoption to larger landholders.

promise_text_en: Provide subsidized high-quality seeds for hemp cultivation to farmers

impact_mechanism: Subsidized high-quality seeds reduce upfront input costs and improve crop viability for marginal hemp farmers.
first_order_effect: Estimated 10,000–20,000 farmers receive subsidized hemp seeds; seed cost reduction ~₹2,000–5,000 per hectare; hemp acreage increases by ~10,000–20,000 hectares.
second_order_effect: Improved seed quality increases germination and yield; however, farmer income gains are offset if hemp output markets are underdeveloped (no guaranteed offtake, volatile prices).
third_order_effect: Long-term impact depends on establishment of hemp processing and export markets; without market linkage, seed subsidy alone cannot sustain income gains, risking farmer debt and crop abandonment.

impact_mechanism: Drip irrigation subsidy reduces capital cost barrier to water-efficient technology adoption, enabling farmers to expand cultivated area and improve yield on water-stressed land.
first_order_effect: Estimated 50,000–100,000 hectares receive drip/subsurface drip irrigation subsidy (₹15–40 cr total subsidy cost); water consumption reduces ~40–50% per hectare; crop yield increases ~20–30%.
second_order_effect: Farmer income from irrigated crops increases by ₹40,000–80,000/hectare/year; groundwater table stabilizes in water-scarce districts; agricultural sustainability improves, reducing climate vulnerability.
third_order_effect: Long-term groundwater recovery enables multi-generational farming viability; reduced water scarcity stress decreases rural-to-urban migration pressure; equitable water sharing reduces inter-village conflict.

promise_text_en: Establish waste management and recycling programs for agricultural residue and promote circular economy in farming

impact_mechanism: Agricultural residue recycling and circular economy promotion converts agricultural waste into income and reduces environmental pollution from crop-burning.
first_order_effect: Estimated 30,000–50,000 farms participate in waste recycling programs; rice straw, sugarcane bagasse, coconut husk converted into biofuel, compost, or animal feed; crop-burning incidents reduce by ~40–50% in participating regions.
second_order_effect: Additional farm income from residue sales (₹10,000–20,000/hectare/year); air quality improves during burning season; soil carbon content increases through compost use, improving soil health and moisture retention.
third_order_effect: Soil improvement reduces fertilizer requirement and input costs over 5–10 years; sustainable farming practices become economically rational; agricultural carbon sequestration contributes to climate commitments; new biomass-based industries emerge (biofuel, biochar production).

promise_text_en: Support sustainable crop residue management and promote bio-based alternatives for fuel, compost and other agricultural byproducts

impact_mechanism: Income diversification and waste utilization through incentivizing conversion of crop residue into marketable bio-based products (fuel, compost) that reduce disposal costs and create secondary revenue streams for farmers.
first_order_effect: Farmers shift from burning residue to collecting and processing it; reduction in air pollution from stubble burning; creation of collection and processing infrastructure in agricultural regions.
second_order_effect: Increased demand for residue-processing equipment and labor creates rural employment; bio-compost production reduces chemical fertilizer dependency, lowering input costs for adopting farmers.
third_order_effect: Soil health improves across regions with sustained compost use, reducing long-term fertilizer subsidy burden on state budget and enabling yield stability without input intensification.

promise_text_en: Develop value-added products from hemp including cosmetics, perfumes and scented products with government support

impact_mechanism: Value-chain extension through government-supported processing and branding of hemp derivatives (cosmetics, perfumes) that captures higher margins than raw hemp fiber, increasing farmer net income per unit cultivated.
first_order_effect: Establishment of hemp processing units and cosmetic formulation facilities; initial cultivation remains low until processing infrastructure and market linkage are operational; pilot batches of value-added products launched.
second_order_effect: If processing succeeds, demand for cultivated hemp increases; farmers in pilot regions expand acreage; supply chain becomes viable only if government absorbs initial losses or guarantees offtake.
third_order_effect: Hemp becomes an alternative cash crop in TN, diversifying farm income away from cotton/sugarcane; however, success depends critically on product-market fit and export viability—high risk of stranded processing assets if cosmetics fail commercially.

promise_text_en: Promote hemp cultivation in coastal and arid regions with specialized government schemes
promise_text_ta: àˆóè£‡†, «ñŸ° õƒè ñ£GôƒèO™ Þ¼Šð¬îŠ «ð£™ îIöèˆF½‹ êí™ ï£˜„ªê® õ÷˜‚è„ CøŠ¹ˆ F†ì‹ ªè£‡´ õóŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Geographic crop diversification through subsidized hemp promotion in climate-marginal (coastal, arid) regions where traditional cereals underperform, enabling cultivation of otherwise fallow or low-productivity land.
first_order_effect: ~50,000–100,000 farmers in coastal (Cuddalore, Tiruvannamalai) and arid (Villupuram, Ranipet) districts receive hemp seeds, saplings, and extension support; initial acreage expansion of ~20,000–30,000 acres by Year 2.
second_order_effect: Hemp cultivation establishes; output is raw fiber or seed. Without guaranteed buyer or price floor, farmers face market risk. Profitability depends on realizing ₹40,000–50,000/acre (global hemp prices volatile).
third_order_effect: If markets hold, arid/coastal regions see sustained income uplift; if buyer network or pricing collapses, returns to fallow land and program credibility damage. Systemic impact weak without value-chain guarantee.

promise_text_en: Provide soil health cards to farmers with soil testing and nutrient recommendations to improve crop yields
promise_text_ta: ñ£õ†ì‰«î£Á‹ î£Qò‚ Aìƒ°
impact_mechanism: Evidence-based input optimization through soil testing and nutrient recommendation cards that reduce fertilizer waste, lower farmer input costs, and improve yield per acre without increasing absolute fertilizer subsidy outlay.
first_order_effect: ~5–8 lakh farmers receive soil health cards and micro-nutrient recommendations; fertilizer application becomes targeted to soil deficiency; fertilizer use efficiency (kg nutrient/kg output) improves by ~10–15%.
second_order_effect: Lower per-acre fertilizer spend reduces input costs; yields remain stable or improve modestly (2–5%) due to balanced nutrient application; farmer net income per acre increases by ~₹1,500–2,500.
third_order_effect: Sustained soil health prevents nutrient depletion and micro-nutrient deficiency; chemical fertilizer subsidy burden declines proportionally; land productivity stabilizes on marginal soils, reducing pressure for agricultural expansion into forests.

promise_text_en: Deploy 1,70,000 micro-irrigation units and provide subsidies up to ₹85,000 per farmer for installation

impact_mechanism: Water access expansion through capital subsidy for micro-irrigation adoption that reduces irrigation cost per hectare, increases cropping intensity, and enables cultivation of water-scarce regions without dependence on monsoon or groundwater depletion.
first_order_effect: 1,70,000 micro-irrigation units (drip/sprinkler) installed; ₹85,000/unit × 1.7L = ₹14,450 cr total subsidy outlay required; ~4–5 lakh farmers gain irrigation access; cropping intensity increases from ~120% to ~150–180% depending on water availability.
second_order_effect: Farmers shift to high-value crops (vegetables, fruits, spices); per-acre income rises 50–80%; groundwater extraction declines, water table stabilization in regions with recharge; secondary employment in drip maintenance and agri-services emerges.
third_order_effect: Sustainable water balance in irrigation commands; reduced subsidy burden on electricity for groundwater pumping; agricultural income decoupling from monsoon variability enables livelihood stability and reduces distress migration.

promise_text_en: Develop 12,00,000 acres with precision irrigation and modern farming techniques

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure-led productivity improvement through precision irrigation and mechanization deployment over 12 lakh acres, lowering per-unit water and labor input while raising yield per hectare across large landscape.
first_order_effect: ~8–12 lakh farmers benefiting from precision irrigation and mechanized farming on 12 lakh acres; irrigation efficiency improves to 75–80%; labor requirement per acre drops 20–30%; average yield gains ~15–25% (crop-specific).
second_order_effect: Per-acre input costs (water, labor) decline; farmer net income rises ₹8,000–15,000/acre; cropping pattern shifts toward higher-value crops; rural unemployment decreases in mechanized operations but labor displacement in manual operations.
third_order_effect: Agricultural output per hectare approaches global benchmarks; TN becomes agro-export competitive; however, labor displacement risk accelerates unless skilling programs redirect displaced workers; land consolidation may increase if marginal farmers cannot adopt precision tech.

promise_text_en: Provide agricultural subsidies between ₹2,000-3,000 per acre for all farmers cultivating supported crops

impact_mechanism: Direct income support through per-acre cash transfer (₹2,000–3,000/acre) that acts as income floor for cultivators, reducing risk of distress sales and enabling working capital retention for next-season inputs.
first_order_effect: ~2–3 crore farmer-seasons (estimated ~1.5 cr farmers, 1.5–2 seasons/year coverage) receive ₹2,000–3,000/acre; ₹30,000–45,000 cr annual expenditure if all TN cultivated area (~1.5 cr acres) is covered; cash flows to ~70% of farming households.
second_order_effect: Farmer cash reserves increase; distress land sales and usury borrowing decline; input purchase capacity improves; consumption expenditure rises, multiplying into rural labor and trade activity.
third_order_effect: Agricultural debt burden stabilizes; rural consumption-led growth strengthens; however, subsidy becomes a permanent entitlement with weak link to productivity—risk of fiscal lock-in without commensurate yield gains or export revenue offset.

promise_text_en: Introduce crop insurance schemes covering losses and ensure support for farmer livelihoods
promise_text_ta: ªïAN ñ£ŸÁ
impact_mechanism: Risk pooling and income protection through subsidized crop insurance that compensates farmers for yield/revenue losses from weather, pests, or price shocks, reducing catastrophic income collapse and enabling borrowing confidence.
first_order_effect: ~1.5–2 crore ration-card farmers enrolled in government-backed crop insurance; premium subsidy ~₹200–300/farmer/season; claim payout (~₹5,000–15,000/hectare) triggered on loss events affecting ~10–15% of insured area annually; ~₹2,000–3,000 cr annual subsidy + payout required.
second_order_effect: Farmer stress borrowing from informal lenders declines; institutional credit access improves (banks more willing to lend to insured farmers); livelihood floor is protected post-loss; debt-driven farm exits reduce.
third_order_effect: Agricultural labor and input supply chains stabilize (less default on debts to seed/fertilizer traders); rural credit markets deepen; however, systemic impact weak if insurance payouts are delayed or claim denials erode trust—requires robust grievance system.

promise_text_en: Promote ethanol production from sugarcane and other biomass sources to create alternate income streams for farmers
promise_text_ta: è¼‹¹ ñŸÁ‹ âKê£ó£ò‹ (Ethanol) à¼õ£‚è‹
impact_mechanism: Diversifies farm revenue by creating a value-added processing pathway for sugarcane biomass, reducing dependence on volatile commodity sugar prices.
first_order_effect: Sugarcane farmers gain access to ethanol production contracts; estimated ~3-4 lakh sugarcane farmers in TN can shift 10-20% of output to ethanol supply chains, generating supplementary income of ₹8,000-15,000/acre/season.
second_order_effect: Increased demand for bagasse and molasses stabilizes agricultural residue pricing; farmers reduce open burning (environmental co-benefit), and ethanol distilleries require skilled labour, creating ~5,000-8,000 rural jobs in processing hubs.
third_order_effect: Long-term: ethanol fuel blend mandates (E5/E10) create structural market pull; TN becomes a renewable fuel production hub, reducing import dependence and establishing a climate-aligned agricultural value chain.

promise_text_en: Establish integrated agricultural extension services with dedicated agricultural officers for each village

impact_mechanism: Decentralizes agricultural knowledge transfer by placing trained extension workers within village reach, reducing information asymmetry and adoption lag for modern farming practices.
first_order_effect: One dedicated agricultural officer deployed per village (~27,000 villages in TN); farmers receive direct access to soil testing, pest management, and crop advisory services without travel; estimated ~1.8-2.0 crore farm households gain regular extension contact (vs. current ~30-40% coverage).
second_order_effect: Farmers adopt improved varieties and practices faster (6-12 month lag reduction); crop yields improve 12-18% in early adopter clusters; knowledge spillover increases adoption rates in non-covered regions.
third_order_effect: Institutionalization of evidence-based farming reduces yield volatility; agricultural labour productivity rises, creating upward wage pressure and reducing seasonal migration dependency.

promise_text_en: Provide agricultural extension support through multiple channels (grants, loans, insurance, credit schemes)
promise_text_ta: å«ó ð‡¬íJ™ ²öŸC º¬øJ™ Þ¬í‚èŠð†´, å¼õ˜ Ü¬ùˆ¶ˆ «î¬õè¬÷»‹ (àí¾, àó‹, âKªð£¼œ, õ¼õ£Œ) ªðŸÁ‚ªè£œÀ‹ õ¬èJ™ «õ÷£‡ º¬ø Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Bundles multiple financial and risk-management instruments to remove access barriers to credit, insurance, and grants for capital-constrained farmers.
first_order_effect: Multi-instrument support (grants for small-scale tech, loans at subsidized rates, insurance bundling) reaches ~80-90 lakh farmers; average farmer household accesses ₹50,000-1.5 lakh in combined support per cycle, unlocking investment in seeds, fertilizer, and equipment.
second_order_effect: Loan uptake increases input use efficiency; insured farmers take on higher-yielding but higher-risk crops; default rates stabilize because income volatility is partially hedged by crop insurance.
third_order_effect: Over 5 years, reduced default and insurance claims accumulation create a positive credit history database; formal agricultural credit deepens, reducing informal moneylending dependency and debt traps.

promise_text_en: Implement integrated agricultural extension for all villages with focus on quality inputs and technology transfer

impact_mechanism: Replicates index 1 promise with explicit focus on input quality and technology diffusion; ensures farmers access certified seeds, quality fertilizers, and modern techniques at village level.
first_order_effect: All 27,000+ villages receive integrated extension with technology transfer modules (drip irrigation, precision farming, soil health cards); ~2.0 crore farm households adopt quality inputs; certified seed adoption rises from ~45% to ~70% coverage.
second_order_effect: Higher seed quality and matched input use reduce crop failure rates by 15-25%; productivity per hectare increases 18-25%; farmer incomes stabilize, reducing distress migration from agricultural families.
third_order_effect: Sustained productivity gains attract youth retention in farming; agricultural labour transitions from wage labour to skill-based roles (seed selection, equipment operation); rural wage structures shift upward.

promise_text_en: Establish milk production standardization with A-1 grade milk quality and support dairy farmers with infrastructure

impact_mechanism: Upgrades dairy farmer productivity and market access by institutionalizing quality standards and infrastructure support, shifting from low-value commodity milk to premium-grade output.
first_order_effect: A-1 grade milk standardization framework established; dairy farmers (~12-15 lakh in TN) receive infrastructure support (cooling tanks, breeding advice, feed supply chains); milk quality improves, enabling premium pricing (+15-20% over standard rates).
second_order_effect: Premium pricing incentivizes herd improvement and animal welfare; dairy co-ops and processors transition to organized supply chains; milk wastage reduces from ~8-10% to ~3-4%; farmer-to-consumer margin compression benefits both groups.
third_order_effect: Formalization of dairy supply chains enables dairy processors to meet export standards; TN dairy exports grow; rural dairy economy integrates into GVC; secondary agri-business (feed, veterinary) sectors expand.

promise_text_en: Provide 6 months of lactation support (₹402-5000) to dairy farmers maintaining A-1 grade milk standards

impact_mechanism: Provides direct income support conditional on milk quality compliance, directly increasing dairy farmer household income during peak production lactation cycles.
first_order_effect: Dairy farmers maintaining A-1 standards receive ₹402-5,000/month for 6 months (~₹2,400-30,000 per lactation cycle); estimated ~8-10 lakh eligible farmers receive support (~₹1,920-3,000 crore annually, depending on take-up); average household income increases 8-12% during support period.
second_order_effect: Predictable income from lactation support incentivizes breed replacement (away from low-yielding stock) and herd maintenance; farmers reduce emergency livestock sales during low-price seasons; animal productivity and welfare improve.
third_order_effect: Sustained lactation income reduces household debt accumulation; female dairy farmers (60-70% of dairy workforce) gain bargaining power in household resource allocation; investment in children's education increases.

promise_text_en: Replace Jersey breed cattle with indigenous A2 milk-producing breeds to eliminate 'Devil in the milk' (A1 beta-casein) and provide healthier milk to consumers
promise_text_ta: è£™ï¬ì «ñô£‡¬ñŠ ð‡¬íèÀ‚° Ü¼A™ Ý†´Š¹¿‚¬è, ñ£†´„ ê£í‹, CÁc˜‚ èN¾èO™ Þ¼‰¶ ä‰¶ ÝM¡ ªð£¼œ (Ý¬ù‰¶), Üºî‚è¬óê™, ñ‡¹¿ àóƒèœ à¼õ£‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Replaces lower-productivity cattle breeds with A2 milk-producing indigenous breeds, eliminating A1 beta-casein (reputedly problematic in modern diets) and raising milk yield per animal.
first_order_effect: Jersey breed cattle (lower A2 milk %) gradually replaced with A2 breeds (e.g., Gir, Sahiwal, Red Sindhi); ~12-15 lakh dairy farmers affected; milk quality shifts from mixed A1/A2 to predominant A2; estimated herd transition over 5-7 years via breeding programs and subsidized animal exchange.
second_order_effect: A2 milk commands premium pricing (+20-30%) in retail/wellness markets; dairy processors market 'A2 pure' branded products; consumer demand stabilizes at higher price; farmers' milk value increases even if yield dips slightly (A2 breeds ~3-3.5 L/day vs. Jersey ~4-5 L/day).
third_order_effect: TN develops 'A2 dairy' market positioning; domestic and export demand for TN 'native breed' milk grows; indigenous cattle breeding becomes economically viable, supporting biodiversity conservation and farmer premium capture.

promise_text_en: Establish dairy cattle breeding programs with improved breeds (A2), maintaining production of 3.5 liters per cow per day
promise_text_ta: îI›ï£†®™ ð£L¡ ê‰¬î ñFŠ¹ êŸªø£Šð 3.5 Þô‚è‹ «è£®
impact_mechanism: Institutionalizes cattle breeding programs that improve animal genetics, ensuring consistent milk yield of 3.5 L/cow/day while upgrading herd productivity across the state.
first_order_effect: Selective breeding programs for A2 breeds established at block/district level; ~12-15 lakh dairy farmers access improved breeding bulls and genetic advisories; herd average milk yield improves from ~2.2-2.5 L/day (current TN average) to ~3.5 L/day over 5-7 years; per-cow annual output increases ~₹15,000-20,000.
second_order_effect: Higher per-animal productivity reduces herd size needed to maintain income; grazing pressure on common lands decreases; feed input cost per litre of milk falls; profitability per cow rises, making dairy viable on smaller landholdings.
third_order_effect: Smaller, higher-productivity herds enable women and marginal farmers to enter dairy without high land/capital barriers; female labour in dairy stabilizes at higher skill levels; dairy sector becomes a key pathway for rural women's income and autonomy.

promise_text_en: Provide comprehensive support to dairy farmers including feed, veterinary care, and marketing assistance for A2 cattle breeds

impact_mechanism: Bundled input subsidy (feed, vet care) + market aggregation reduces per-unit production costs and transaction friction for dairy farmers, enabling higher net income per animal.
first_order_effect: Dairy farmers operating A2 cattle breeds gain access to subsidized feed, preventive/curative veterinary services, and assured marketing channels; mortality and disease-related production loss decline; ~8–12 lakh small/marginal dairy farmers in TN benefit if coverage is near-universal.
second_order_effect: Lower per-unit production costs and reduced animal loss increase milk yield per farmer and profit margins; farmers reinvest in herd expansion or land improvement, raising dairy sector productivity and farmer household income by 15–25%.
third_order_effect: Sustained income growth encourages younger farmers to remain in dairy; rural asset accumulation rises; local feed/vet supply chains formalize and grow, creating employment in rural supply chains and reducing urban migration pressure.

impact_mechanism: Value-added processing subsidy (cheese production) diverts raw milk margin from middlemen to farmer cooperatives, allowing farmers to capture 40–60% of retail margin instead of 10–15% on fluid milk.
first_order_effect: Cooperative dairy units adopt cheese production; if 2,000–3,000 units are supported, 10,000–15,000 farmer families gain secondary income stream; gross margin per unit rises from ~₹15–20 lakhs (fluid milk) to ₹40–60 lakhs (cheese + fluid sales combined).
second_order_effect: Higher per-unit profit incentivizes consolidation of small producers into cooperative processing units; milk collection volume increases; cooperative balance sheets strengthen, enabling reinvestment in cold chain and packaging infrastructure.
third_order_effect: Formalized cooperative processing creates employment in production, quality control, and distribution; rural value chain locks in farmer participation; cheese becomes exportable commodity, linking TN dairy to state/national/export markets.

promise_text_en: Implement a 'Green Revolution' scheme providing subsidies of ₹15,000 to ₹25,000 per unit for dairy farming equipment

impact_mechanism: Capital subsidy (₹15k–₹25k per unit) reduces upfront equipment cost barrier, enabling small farmers to adopt mechanized milking, chilling, and storage equipment without debt.
first_order_effect: If ~2 lakh farmers adopt equipment, aggregate subsidy outlay ~₹300–500 cr; milking labor per farm falls 30–40%; milk quality (somatic cell count, bacterial load) improves, enabling higher procurement prices; net income per cow rises ₹3,000–₹8,000 annually.
second_order_effect: Farmers reallocate labor time from milking to herd health and breeding; herd reproduction cycles shorten; culling of low-yield animals accelerates; average herd productivity rises 20–30%; some farmers expand herds using saved labor costs.
third_order_effect: Sector-wide productivity gain reduces per-liter production cost; cooperative milk collection efficiency improves; margins available for farmer pricing power widen; steady-state herd size may stabilize or shift toward quality over quantity.

promise_text_en: Provide training on advanced dairy farming techniques and sustainable animal husbandry practices to farmers

impact_mechanism: Skill-building intervention (extension training) raises farmer technical knowledge (breeding, nutrition, disease management), enabling better herd management decisions without external input subsidies.
first_order_effect: If 50,000–100,000 dairy farmers participate in structured training, adoption of practices (improved breeding, silage-making, fodder rotation, vaccination schedules) rises 25–40%; disease prevalence and mortality decline; milk yield per animal increases 10–15% within 12–24 months.
second_order_effect: Trained farmers make higher-quality inputs purchasing decisions; cooperative vet clinics see higher uptake; farmer-to-farmer knowledge transfer accelerates; cooperatives reduce advisory cost per farmer; knowledge stickiness (behavior change persistence) increases relative to one-off subsidy.
third_order_effect: Farmer agency and confidence increase; long-term productivity gains become independent of subsidy dependence; cooperative governance quality improves as trained farmers participate more actively in management; sector innovation capacity rises.

impact_mechanism: Cooperative infrastructure (processing, cold storage, direct market linkages) eliminates middleman markup and price volatility, allowing farmers to capture 35–50% of consumer price instead of current 20–25%.
first_order_effect: If 5,000–8,000 cooperative dairy societies are established or upgraded with processing/cold-chain infrastructure, ~40–60 lakh farmers gain direct market access; transaction costs per liter fall ₹2–₹3; farmer milk price premium rises ₹2–₹4 per liter; cooperative turnover rises.
second_order_effect: Higher milk prices incentivize 15–25% of smallholders to increase production; cooperative collection volumes and processing capacity rise; cooperatives accumulate retained earnings, enabling reinvestment in quality testing, packaging, and retail channels without government subsidy.
third_order_effect: Cooperatives become self-sustaining commercial entities; farmer dependency on government subsidy decreases; formal credit availability to cooperatives expands; competitive dairy market develops with multiple cooperative processors bidding for farmer milk, consolidating price gains.

impact_mechanism: Long-term subsidy guarantee (15 years) + procurement assurance removes price risk and volume uncertainty, enabling cooperatives to invest in processing capacity and farmers to expand production with confidence.
first_order_effect: Cooperative societies receive guaranteed subsidy cover and procurement promise for 15 years; capital-intensive processing investments (cheese, yogurt, ghee units) become bankable; ~50,000–100,000 farmers commit to production expansion; aggregate milk procurement volumes rise 30–40% over 5–7 years.
second_order_effect: Predictable cashflow enables cooperatives to invest in equipment, talent, and market development; milk collection per village rises; farmers' household income stabilizes, reducing distress migration; land under dairy-linked fodder cultivation increases; cooperative employment grows.
third_order_effect: Fiscal commitment of ₹10,000–₹20,000 cr over 15 years locks government into dairy support; dairy becomes political economy linchpin; potential risk: subsidy dependency persists; cooperatives may under-invest in efficiency if subsidy shield remains indefinite.

second_order_effect: Lower disease burden + higher reproduction rates increase calving rates and milk-yielding herd size; farmers perceive vet services as trustworthy and accessible; preventive care demand grows; cooperative vet clinic utilization rates rise to 80–90%; vet supply chain (vaccines, medicines, AI kits) formalizes and scales.
third_order_effect: Sustained herd health improvement enables farmers to phase out informal/unqualified animal treatment; animal welfare standards rise; milk quality (SCC, pathogens) improves, enabling premium pricing; vet profession becomes formalized rural employment destination; baseline animal health metrics improve across state.

impact_mechanism: High-spec veterinary dispensary at block level (diagnostic imaging, lab capacity) enables rapid, accurate diagnosis of complex animal health issues, reducing treatment delay and misdiagnosis-driven mortality in pastoral and smallholder herds.
first_order_effect: ~1,000 block-level dispensaries established with MRI, ECG, blood test capacity; ~3,000 veterinarians deployed; diagnostic capacity increases from near-zero to serve 30,000+ animals per block per year; complex health cases (lameness, reproductive failure, systemic infections) achieve correct diagnosis and treatment within 24–48 hours vs. current 5–7 day delay.
second_order_effect: Diagnostic accuracy rises 70–80%; farmer confidence in vet system strengthens; treatment success rates improve; average disease recovery time halves; productivity loss from prolonged illness declines; veterinary profession attracts better talent due to modern infrastructure.
third_order_effect: Animal health data (diagnostic patterns, disease prevalence) becomes centralized and analyzable; state livestock dept. can conduct epidemiological surveillance and design targeted prevention; herd-level health metrics improve; potential for data-driven animal insurance product development.

impact_mechanism: Proximity-based access to veterinary expertise reduces disease-driven livestock mortality and enables farmers to adopt improved breeding and nutrition practices.
first_order_effect: One animal husbandry clinic established per district (~38 clinics) provides veterinary care, nutrition counseling, and breed improvement services to livestock-dependent smallholders and marginal farmers across TN.
second_order_effect: Reduced livestock morbidity and mortality improves milk/meat productivity per animal, increasing household dairy/pastoral income by 15-25% within 2-3 years, particularly for ~50 lakh rural households with 1-5 animals.
third_order_effect: Improved livestock productivity anchors a secondary income stream for marginal farmers, reducing dependence on seasonal crop income and enabling reinvestment in farm inputs or education.

Ensure convergence of all available veterinary services including basic clinical care, diagnostic facilities, advanced surgical procedures, and emergency services across traditional and modern veterinary systems.

impact_mechanism: Convergence of modern and traditional veterinary services at scale ensures disease detection, emergency response, and preventive care reach scattered rural livestock populations within serviceable time.
first_order_effect: Existing veterinary infrastructure (clinics, hospitals, diagnostic labs) is upgraded with surgical capacity, diagnostic equipment, and trained staff; emergency services (24/7 callouts) are operationalized across districts.
second_order_effect: Faster diagnosis and treatment of acute animal illnesses (foot-and-mouth, mastitis, pneumonia) reduces herd losses and treatment costs for farmers; diagnostic capacity enables early detection of zoonotic risks.
third_order_effect: Livestock disease burden at state level declines; animal-origin disease outbreaks are contained faster, protecting both animal agriculture income and public health.

second_order_effect: Livestock mortality from treatable acute conditions (dystocia, acute infection, injury) declines by 20-30%; farmers avoid catastrophic loss of productive animals, stabilizing household income.
third_order_effect: Cooperative societies become primary veterinary access points, strengthening their role as farm service intermediaries and increasing farmer reliance on institutional credit/input channels.

second_order_effect: Shepherd and pastoral households (est. 5-8 lakh across TN) gain reliable access to animal health services; herd productivity improves, reducing pressure to overgraze or migrate in search of veterinary care.
third_order_effect: Pastoral livelihoods stabilize; grazing-induced land degradation pressure eases in fragile zones; community ties to land tenure strengthen.

second_order_effect: Soil health improves under organic management (lower chemical input costs); millet cultivation returns water-scarce districts to traditional drought-resistant crops; horticulture yields higher per-hectare income (3-5x commodity crops) but requires 2-3 years establishment.
third_order_effect: Agricultural income volatility decreases for adopting farmers; agro-ecological resilience to drought/erratic rainfall increases; chemical subsidy burden on state budget declines.

second_order_effect: Complex surgical cases (fracture repair, tumor removal, cesarean delivery) are treatable in-state, eliminating farmer losses from euthanasia of valuable breeding animals; disease outbreaks are contained faster with diagnostic capacity.
third_order_effect: Livestock breeding stock quality improves across TN; farmers retain productive animals longer; genetic improvement programs become feasible.

impact_mechanism: Cross-departmental coordination framework (TRANSTAN) enables real-time disease surveillance, resource sharing, and unified response protocols across veterinary departments.
first_order_effect: A trans-state veterinary network is operationalized with shared data systems, unified protocols for disease reporting, and joint rapid-response teams; veterinary departments across districts are linked in a coordinated surveillance system.
second_order_effect: Animal disease outbreaks are detected earlier and contained faster through coordinated quarantine and culling; zoonotic risks are flagged for public health intervention; veterinary resources are allocated efficiently to hotspots.
third_order_effect: State-level livestock disease burden and volatility decline; investment confidence in animal agriculture increases; interstate disease transmission risk reduces.

impact_mechanism: Subsidized surgical equipment (implants, plates, orthotic devices) lowers treatment costs for fractures and orthopedic conditions in animals, enabling corrective rather than euthanasia outcomes.

second_order_effect: Farmers with valuable breeding animals (dairy cows, bulls, sheep) opt for surgical repair instead of culling; productive lifespan of animals extends by 2-4 years; household income from these animals is preserved.
third_order_effect: Herd replacement frequency decreases; farmers reduce pressure to breed replacements, allowing improved genetic selection; veterinary service utilization and surgical expertise deepen across districts.

impact_mechanism: Round-the-clock veterinary service provision reduces livestock mortality and morbidity through timely emergency intervention, protecting farm household income and asset base.
first_order_effect: Livestock farmers gain access to emergency veterinary care within response time; reduced animal deaths during off-hours and in remote areas (~3-4 crore livestock owners across TN benefit).
second_order_effect: Lower livestock mortality increases herd productivity and milk/meat output; farmers retain higher income from each animal cycle, reducing need for distress asset sales.
third_order_effect: Sustained livestock income enables farmer households to invest in land improvement, children's education, and weather resilience; reduces seasonal debt cycles in pastoral communities.

promise_text_en: Improve disease surveillance and animal health monitoring systems in veterinary hospitals across all districts

impact_mechanism: Strengthened disease surveillance systems enable early outbreak detection and containment, preventing large-scale livestock epidemics that devastate farm incomes.
first_order_effect: District veterinary hospitals implement real-time disease monitoring; faster identification of foot-and-mouth, lumpy skin, and poultry diseases across all 38 TN districts.
second_order_effect: Early quarantine and treatment prevent epidemic spread; herd/flock mortality rates decline, protecting collective livestock wealth and reducing indirect income loss across supply chains.
third_order_effect: Predictable animal health reduces farm input costs and price volatility; enables farmers to engage in forward contracts with processors, increasing income stability.

impact_mechanism: Free advanced prosthetics (bionic limbs) reduce mobility barriers and restore functional capacity for ~15-20 lakh persons with limb disabilities, enabling employment and social participation.
first_order_effect: Eligible disabled persons receive fitted bionic arms/fiber legs at no cost; immediate restoration of mobility and upper-body function for activities of daily living and work.
second_order_effect: Improved mobility increases labor force participation and reduces caregiver dependency; disabled individuals access employment (formal/informal) and earn household income.
third_order_effect: Higher workforce participation of PWDs reduces overall household poverty rates; increased economic self-sufficiency reduces long-term welfare dependency and burden on family support systems.

promise_text_en: Provide ambulance services to every person at the time of medical emergency

impact_mechanism: Universal ambulance access removes transport barriers to emergency care, enabling timely hospital admission for acute conditions and reducing preventable deaths.
first_order_effect: Every person in medical emergency (cardiac, trauma, obstetric, acute illness) receives free ambulance transport to hospital; response time for emergency cases falls from hours to minutes in urban/semi-urban areas.
second_order_effect: Faster hospital admission increases survival rates for time-critical conditions (MI, stroke, complications); reduces in-transit mortality and permanent disability from delays.
third_order_effect: Reduced emergency mortality burden decreases household catastrophic expenses from prolonged illness; enables workforce participation and prevents family debt spirals from medical emergency.

impact_mechanism: Free secondary medical opinion (expert consultation) reduces diagnostic error and inappropriate treatment, improving outcomes and preventing wasteful/harmful interventions for disabled persons.
first_order_effect: Disabled persons with complex conditions access free expert review of primary diagnosis/treatment; second opinions identify misdiagnoses and alternative therapeutic pathways at no cost.
second_order_effect: Reduced diagnostic errors prevent prolonged ineffective treatments and associated out-of-pocket costs; disabled persons avoid unnecessary procedures and receive evidence-based care.
third_order_effect: Lower treatment costs and better outcomes reduce catastrophic health expenditure for PWD households; enables continued investment in disability aids and rehabilitation rather than debt servicing.

impact_mechanism: Provision of 42 additional assistive devices (mobility aids, hearing aids, visual aids, communication devices) through government health university expands access to adaptive equipment for persons with disabilities.

second_order_effect: Broader device availability increases functional capacity across disability types (mobility, sensory, communication); enables participation in education, work, and social activities previously inaccessible.
third_order_effect: Improved functional participation increases school enrollment, employment rates, and social integration among PWDs; reduces long-term dependency and contributes to workforce productivity.

second_order_effect: Successful regeneration reduces disability severity; previously immobile or paralyzed individuals regain limb function, speech, or sensory capacity, expanding work and social capacity.
third_order_effect: Functional restoration enables workforce re-entry and reduced dependency; however, benefit is conditional on treatment success (highly variable) and access limited to suitable candidates.

promise_text_en: Establish rehabilitation programs including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy for disabled children and ensure accessibility

impact_mechanism: Early intensive rehabilitation (physiotherapy, occupational, speech therapy) for disabled children normalizes development trajectories and prevents secondary disability, enabling mainstream education and social integration.
first_order_effect: Disabled children aged 0-18 access multi-disciplinary rehab services (PT, OT, speech) through government programs; functional improvements in motor control, speech, self-care skills within 12-24 months of therapy.
second_order_effect: Improved functional capacity enables school enrollment and peer interaction; disabled children transition from isolation to mainstream education, reducing social stigma and psychological burden on families.
third_order_effect: Mainstream education access increases educational attainment and employment prospects in adulthood; reduces lifetime poverty risk and enables disabled cohort to become economically active, not dependent.

promise_text_en: Provide free information and communication technology training to disabled persons to enhance employment opportunities

impact_mechanism: Skill building via subsidized ICT training lowers employment barriers for disabled persons by providing job-ready technical qualifications.
first_order_effect: Disabled persons (estimated ~15-20 lakh in TN with employment age) gain certified ICT skills; training completion rates likely 40-60% depending on accessibility of training centres and trainer availability.
second_order_effect: Increased job placement in BPO, IT, data entry, and remote work roles; however, labour market absorption depends on employer willingness to hire disabled workers—without complementary anti-discrimination enforcement or reservation mandates, placement gains may stall at 20-30% of trainees.
third_order_effect: If paired with enforcement of disability hiring quotas and workplace accessibility standards, sustained wage income for disabled workers could reduce poverty incidence in this group by 15-25% over 10 years; without such pairing, effect remains marginal.

promise_text_en: Establish comprehensive disability support covering multiple types of disabilities including cerebral palsy, autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disability, and hearing impairment
