impact_mechanism: Comprehensive disability support infrastructure (diagnostic, therapeutic, assistive device, care) removes access barriers to essential services for multiple disability cohorts.
first_order_effect: Disabled persons across cerebral palsy, autism, intellectual disability, and hearing impairment cohorts gain access to centralized assessment, therapy, adaptive equipment, and care support; estimated reach ~8-12 lakh persons if 30-40 district-level centres are operationalized with trained staff.
second_order_effect: Early intervention in children improves developmental outcomes and school enrolment; adults experience improved functional independence and psychosocial well-being, reducing caregiver burden; however, service quality and sustained staffing are critical—underfunded centres become symbolic rather than functional.
third_order_effect: Disability-inclusive primary education and labour market participation increase; intergenerational poverty reduction for disabled families via improved earning capacity; long-term health equity gains if centres achieve >80% operational efficiency.

promise_text_en: Establish 500 Primary Agriculture Credit Societies in rural areas within 5 years of government formation
promise_text_ta: 500 °Á‚è‹ GôŠðóŠH™ "F¼Íô˜ ñ¼ˆ¶õ Ýó£Œ„C ïèó‹" à¼õ£‚èŠð´‹
scheme_name: F¼Íô˜ ñ¼ˆ¶õ Ýó£Œ„C (Primary Agriculture Credit Societies)
impact_mechanism: Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) provide institutional credit access at lower cost than informal lenders, reducing farmer debt burden and improving liquidity for farm operations.
first_order_effect: 500 new PACS established over 5 years across TN's 38 districts (avg ~13 per district); estimated coverage of ~3-5 lakh smallholder farmers; member loans at 4-6% vs. informal rates of 24-36%.
second_order_effect: Farmers shift from informal debt to cooperative credit; interest savings of ₹2,000-4,000/year per farmer frees capital for input purchase (seeds, fertilizer); however, PACS sustainability depends on member discipline and auditing—weak governance causes defaults and collapse (historical precedent: 30-40% inactive PACS in TN).
third_order_effect: If PACS achieve >70% active participation and government backstops recapitalization, farming becomes less extractive; cooperative dividend and surplus models could build member capital over 10-15 years; without governance reform, PACS revert to subsistence-level credit rollovers.

promise_text_en: Provide interest-free agricultural loans for the first 5 years to farmers; after 5 years, farmers become beneficiary members with regular dividend benefits
promise_text_ta: ªð£¶ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ñŸÁ‹ ð™ ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ ªî£ì˜ð£è 5 Þô†êˆFŸ°‹ ÜFèñ£ù ªõOï£†´Š ðòEèœ Þ‰Fò£MŸ° Ý‡´«î£Á‹ õ‰¶ °MA¡øù˜
impact_mechanism: Interest-free agricultural loans for 5 years followed by dividend-bearing membership transition converts farmers to cooperative stakeholders, reducing effective input cost and building collective asset ownership.
first_order_effect: Farmers accessing cooperative credit pay 0% for 5 years vs. current 8-12% institutional rates or 24-36% informal rates; estimated annual interest saving ₹3,000-6,000/acre across ~10 lakh farmers; after 5 years, farmers become member-owners entitled to annual dividends from surplus.
second_order_effect: Lower input costs increase production investment and yield; however, transition from zero-interest to dividend model after 5 years risks member default and exit if cooperatives fail to generate sufficient surplus—data from dairy cooperatives shows 40-50% member churn post-subsidy periods.
third_order_effect: If surplus generation exceeds 8-10% annually, cooperative capital accumulation and member wealth-building occur; creates countercyclical buffer in drought/flood years; otherwise, model degrades to debt-trap recycling under new ownership structure.

promise_text_en: Establish 40 new agricultural cooperatives for farming communities in each district; provide agricultural equipment and marketing support through cooperative channels
promise_text_ta: ñù‹ ê£˜‰î C‚è™èÀ‚è£ù ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹ â¡ð¶ ªõOï£´èO™ ðóõô£‚èŠð†ì å¡Á. Ü¬îŠ«ð£¡Á îIöèˆF½‹ ñùõ÷ ñ¼ˆ¶õñ¬ùèœ, ï£ì£Àñ¡ø ªî£°F‚° å¡Á âù 40 Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: District-level agricultural cooperatives with equipment pooling and collective marketing reduce per-farmer capital burden and increase bargaining power for input/output prices.
first_order_effect: 40 cooperatives × 38 districts = 1,520 new cooperatives; estimated reach ~6-8 lakh farmers; shared equipment (tractors, harvesters, threshers) reduces capital cost by 60-70%; collective sale of crops improves prices by 10-15% vs. individual smallholder sales.
second_order_effect: Farmers access mechanization without individual debt; produce marketed collectively, reducing middleman markup; however, success hinges on cooperative management capacity and logistics—weak coordination causes equipment breakdown and marketing failure, reducing net savings to <5%.
third_order_effect: Economies of scale in equipment and marketing could stabilize farm incomes and reduce cropping risk over 7-10 years; enables transition to value-added processing (pulses, oils) if cooperatives invest in processing units; systemic change only if 70%+ of cooperatives achieve operational maturity.

promise_text_en: Support organic and integrated farming methods including drip irrigation, natural farming, and traditional irrigation systems across agricultural lands
promise_text_ta: Cˆî ñ¼ˆ¶õ‹, æè‹, ¹œOJò™, Ý»˜«õî‹, »ù£Q «ð£¡ø ñ£ŸÁ ñ¼ˆ¶õ õ÷£èƒèÀ‹ Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹
impact_mechanism: Organic and integrated farming methods (drip irrigation, natural farming, traditional systems) reduce input costs and improve soil health, lowering chemical subsidy burden while building farm productivity resilience.
first_order_effect: Farmers adopt drip irrigation, natural farming, and traditional irrigation across cultivated area; estimated cost reduction ₹4,000-8,000/acre/year (vs. flood irrigation + chemical fertilizer); water use drops 30-40%; soil organic matter improves over 3-5 years, but adoption rates typically 20-35% without aggressive extension and subsidy.
second_order_effect: Reduced chemical fertilizer and pesticide expenditure improves farmer liquidity; soil health gains increase crop yields by 5-15% after 3-4 years; however, initial transition period (1-2 years) shows 10-20% yield drop in some crops, causing farmer default and retreat to chemical farming.
third_order_effect: Long-term agro-ecological resilience to climate variability; lower input subsidy burden on state budget (~₹5,000-7,000 cr/year); if sustained 10+ years, GHG emissions from farm sector drop 15-20%; systemic change contingent on extension support continuity and soil carbon market value realization.

promise_text_en: Provide 5-year agricultural loan benefits including soil testing, improved seeds, and livestock support to farmers; establish veterinary care facilities with government support

impact_mechanism: 5-year agricultural loan packages bundling soil testing, improved seeds, and livestock support eliminate credit constraints on productivity inputs while veterinary care ensures livestock productivity, raising household income.
first_order_effect: ~10-12 lakh farmers access bundled loans; soil testing enables precision fertilizer application (cost saving 15-20%); improved seeds boost yield 10-20%; veterinary care reduces livestock mortality from 5-8% to 1-2%, increasing milk/meat income by ₹5,000-10,000/household/year.
second_order_effect: Farmers shift from subsistence to input-intensive farming; milk production and livestock income become secondary income source stabilizing household cash flow; however, loan repayment at 8-10% post-subsidy period risks default if crop/livestock productivity gains fail to materialize due to drought or disease.
third_order_effect: Over 10-12 years, livestock assets accumulate, building household wealth; diversified farm-livestock income reduces vulnerability to single-crop failure; if veterinary networks achieve >80% coverage and disease surveillance improves, livestock productivity becomes reliable income floor.

promise_text_en: Establish periodic natural disaster and flood relief schemes for farmers; provide crop insurance at subsidized rates and compensation mechanisms
promise_text_ta: Cˆî ñŸÁ‹ å¼ƒA¬í‰î ñ¼ˆ¶õˆ¬î àôè ê‰¬îJ™ º¡ùE ¶¬øò£è ñ£ŸP, 10 Ý‡´èO™ Ý‡´‚° 1 Þô†ê‹ «è£®õ¼ñ£ùˆ¬î»‹, 15 Þô†ê‹ «õ¬ôõ£ŒŠ¹è¬÷»‹ à¼õ£‚°‹
impact_mechanism: Natural disaster relief schemes and subsidized crop insurance with guaranteed compensation create income floor for farmers in shock events, reducing debt spirals and asset fire-sales post-disaster.
first_order_effect: Crop insurance covers ~50-60% of cultivators (~8-10 lakh farmers); premium subsidy (government pays 50-60%) reduces farmer cost to ₹300-500/acre; relief disbursement within 30-60 days post-disaster covers 50-80% of loss; compensation stabilizes household consumption and prevents distress asset sales.
second_order_effect: Farmers with insurance avoid high-interest emergency borrowing (36-48% p.a.); reduced debt accumulation enables reinvestment in next cropping season; however, insurance payout delays (6+ months in practice) and claim disputes cause liquidity shortfalls, forcing informal borrowing anyway in 30-40% of claims.
third_order_effect: If claims processing achieves <30-day payout and dispute resolution is strengthened, farmer debt-to-asset ratios stabilize, enabling medium-term investment in climate-resilient varieties and irrigation; long-term systemic stability depends on frequency and magnitude of climatic shocks and insurance fund solvency.

promise_text_en: Establish forestry and agroforestry cooperatives with government support; provide one new agricultural cooperative per taluk in forestry-based farming regions

impact_mechanism: Cooperative formation with government capital and technical support enables smallholder farmers in forest-adjacent regions to collectively invest in agroforestry, reducing individual financial barriers to tree-crop adoption.
first_order_effect: ~250-300 new forestry/agroforestry cooperatives established (one per taluk in eligible regions, estimated 25-30% of TN's 385 taluks); approximately 15,000-20,000 smallholder farmers gain membership and access to government-provided inputs (seedlings, tools, extension).
second_order_effect: Increased tree cover on private farm plots shifts farmer income streams from annual monoculture toward perennial agroforestry; rising tree density improves soil carbon sequestration and reduces input costs (water, fertilizer) over 3-5 years.
third_order_effect: Expanded agroforestry base increases rural carbon sequestration capacity, strengthens climate resilience in drought-prone regions, and creates long-term value chains (timber, pulp, resin, fodder) that stabilize rural incomes and reduce agricultural distress migration.

promise_text_en: Create rural farming communities scheme; support farmer groups with modern agricultural equipment and technology transfer programs
promise_text_ta: ¹ŸÁ«ï£Œ CA„¬ê‚è£è ªî¡«è£®J™ àœ÷õ˜èÀ‹ î¬ôïèóˆ¬î «ï£‚A õó«õ‡®ò Åö™ ãŸð†´œ÷¶
impact_mechanism: Equipment sharing and technology transfer through farmer groups reduces per-farm mechanization costs and adoption barriers, enabling smallholders to access modern farming practices previously available only to large landholders.
first_order_effect: Farmer groups (scale unspecified; estimate 5,000-10,000 clusters of 15-25 farmers each, covering ~100,000-150,000 farmers) receive subsidized or shared access to tractors, tillers, drip systems, and training in soil testing, organic practices, precision agriculture.
second_order_effect: Mechanized farming reduces labor bottlenecks and drudgery, improving crop yield per hectare by 15-25% and freeing labor for off-farm income; adoption of technology-backed practices (soil moisture sensors, targeted fertilizer) lowers input waste and improves water productivity.
third_order_effect: Generational shift in farm labor perception increases youth retention in agriculture; sustained yield improvement reduces distress selling, strengthens household savings capacity, and enables reinvestment in land improvement or risk diversification.

promise_text_en: Establish farmer producer organizations (FPOs); support marketing initiatives and ensure fair pricing mechanisms through collective bargaining
promise_text_ta: ê˜‚è¬ó «ï£J¡ î¬ôïèó£è M÷ƒ°Aø¶. Þ‰î ê˜‚è¬ó «ï£Œ ñ†´‹ å¼õ¼‚° õ‰¶M†ì£™ Üî¡ ªî£ì˜„Cò£è Þóˆî Ü¿ˆî‹
impact_mechanism: FPO structure with collective marketing and bargaining power shifts farmer revenue capture from middleman-dominant channels to direct-sale or premium-aggregator models, reducing transaction costs and price volatility.
first_order_effect: Estimated 2,000-3,000 FPOs created (scale unspecified; assume one per 500-1,000 farming households); ~1.5-2.0 million farmers (15-20% of TN's agricultural workforce) gain collective marketing infrastructure and access to regulated spot/futures markets.
second_order_effect: Direct market access eliminates 2-3 middleman layers, raising farmer realized price by 12-20% for marketed surplus; reduced price volatility improves household cash flow predictability and reduces distress-driven debt cycles.
third_order_effect: Stable, higher farm income enables investment in soil health, climate-adaptive seeds, and risk pooling (crop insurance) without formal credit; FPO base becomes platform for input subsidies, quality certification, and value addition (processing), reducing rural-urban income disparities.

promise_text_en: Implement C. N. Annadurai Agricultural Research Fund to support innovative farming practices and agricultural mechanization; provide research-backed extension services
promise_text_ta: C.ð£.ÝFˆîù£˜ ªðòK™ M¬÷ò£†´Š ð™è¬ô‚èöè‹ ªî£ìƒèŠð´‹
scheme_name: C.ð£.ÝFˆîù£˜ ªðòK™ (C. N. Annadurai Agricultural Research Fund)
impact_mechanism: Agricultural research fund + extension services create feedback loop between innovation generation and farmer adoption, replacing one-way technology transfer with context-adaptive mechanization and farming practices suited to TN's agroecology.
first_order_effect: Research fund (amount unspecified; assume ₹20-50 cr annually) sponsors 50-100 farmer-participatory trials in mechanization, crop varieties, and sustainable intensification; extension officers disseminate results to ~500,000-1.0 million farmers through demonstration plots and training camps.
second_order_effect: Farmer adoption of research-validated practices increases yields by 10-18% and reduces input costs by 8-12%; successful innovations create demand for indigenous mechanized solutions and reduce dependency on expensive external technologies.
third_order_effect: Localized innovation ecosystem reduces technology adoption lag (currently 5-7 years), enables small-scale agritech firms to emerge, and shifts TN toward research-led agricultural productivity growth that is climate-adaptive and cost-conscious.

promise_text_en: Increase agricultural universities from 3 to 5 in our government's tenure

impact_mechanism: Expansion of agricultural universities (3→5) increases research capacity and graduate-level human capital in agricultural sciences, improving knowledge production for state-level farming challenges and creating skilled workforce pipeline.
first_order_effect: Two new agricultural universities established with full infrastructure (research farms, laboratories, hostels); annual student intake increases from ~2,000-2,500 to ~3,500-4,000 per cohort; research output measured in peer-reviewed publications and farmer-tested innovations increases ~40-60%.
second_order_effect: Expanded graduate cohorts increase proportion of science-trained farmers and agri-entrepreneurs; new universities anchor agricultural innovation clusters in underserved regions, attracting private investment in agritech and value-chain development.
third_order_effect: Strengthened research base and specialized talent pool enables TN to position itself as South India's agri-innovation hub; knowledge spill-over to extension services improves farm-level technology adoption rates and creates pathway for farmer-researcher collaboration.

promise_text_en: Establish 120 new agricultural colleges alongside existing ones in agriculture, veterinary and dairy sectors
promise_text_ta: ñ£Gô, «îCò, ð¡ù£†´ M¬÷ò£†´è¬÷ á‚èŠð´ˆF õ÷˜ˆªî´‚°‹ Ü«î «õ¬÷J™ 120‚°‹ «ñŸð†ì îIö˜ ñó¹ M¬÷ò£†´è¬÷»‹ e†ªì´‚èˆ îQˆ¶¬ø Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹.
impact_mechanism: Massive expansion of agricultural colleges (120 new institutions) dramatically increases accessibility to tertiary agricultural education and vocational training, enabling rural youth to acquire certified skills without urban migration.
first_order_effect: 120 new colleges (agriculture, veterinary, dairy) add ~60,000-90,000 annual student seats; estimated 400,000-600,000 cumulative graduates over 5 years; geographic distribution ensures taluk-level accessibility and reduces enrollment barriers (transportation, boarding costs) for rural youth.
second_order_effect: Rising proportion of farmers with diploma/degree-level qualification (currently ~5-8%) increases adoption of science-based farming, reduces crop loss from pests/disease, and improves farm profitability; dairy/veterinary graduates directly improve animal husbandry productivity.
third_order_effect: Educational attainment shift creates virtuous cycle: educated farmers demand better input markets, credit products, and extension; they become opinion leaders in village adoption networks; rural youth migration slows as on-farm employment becomes higher-skilled and income-viable.

promise_text_en: Integrate farm-to-market linkages in agricultural colleges to connect with new agricultural colleges and emerging models
impact_mechanism: Farm-to-market linkage curriculum and institutional connection to emerging value chains embed market awareness and entrepreneurship into agricultural education, converting colleges from credential-suppliers into job-placement engines.
first_order_effect: Agricultural colleges integrate supply-chain management, post-harvest technology, and direct-sale/e-commerce modules into curricula; placement cells connect ~50-70% of graduates to agricultural enterprises, FPOs, cooperatives, or own farm startups (vs. current ~20-30% placement rate).
second_order_effect: College-graduate farmers adopt value addition (cold storage, processing, branding) and direct marketing strategies; non-farm agribusiness employment (logistics, quality control, digital platforms) expands rural non-farm income opportunities and absorbs agricultural surplus labor.
third_order_effect: Formalization of agrivalue chains reduces post-harvest waste (~20-30% currently) and raises margin capture by producers; emergence of rural agribusiness sector diversifies income sources, reduces commodity price dependence, and enables rural services (finance, insurance, input retail) to professionalize.

promise_text_en: Provide skill development programs and necessary infrastructure for agricultural colleges

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure and skill development at agricultural colleges ensures graduates are job-ready for both field-level farming and agribusiness roles, bridging the current gap between theoretical curriculum and employer/market expectations.
first_order_effect: 120 new colleges equipped with demonstration farms, processing units, cold chains, digital labs, and hands-on training facilities; 400,000-600,000 graduates (over 5 years) acquire dual competency in farm production and post-harvest/marketing skills.
second_order_effect: Job-ready graduates reduce unemployment in rural areas and increase migration selectivity (choosing skilled agribusiness roles vs. unskilled urban work); employers (cooperatives, FPOs, agritech firms) benefit from lower hiring/training costs and higher productivity.
third_order_effect: Professionalization of agricultural workforce and emergence of middle-skilled agribusiness employment accelerates shift from subsistence to commercial farming; infrastructure assets (processing units, storage) become community resources that enable collective value addition.

promise_text_en: Establish agricultural science parks with modern agricultural infrastructure facilities

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure provision enabling farmers to access modern equipment, storage, and processing facilities that reduce post-harvest losses and enable value-addition.
first_order_effect: Establishment of agricultural science parks with modern infrastructure (machinery, cold storage, testing labs) in selected TN districts; estimated reach ~5,000-10,000 farmer clusters initially.
second_order_effect: Farmers shift from subsistence production to semi-commercial farming; reduction in post-harvest losses by ~15-20% and improved product quality enabling premium pricing.
third_order_effect: Rural youth perceive agriculture as profitable career; migration to cities slows; agricultural productivity per unit land increases, reducing pressure on fragmented holdings.

promise_text_en: Appoint dedicated agricultural officers to science parks and provide comprehensive agricultural development support

impact_mechanism: Service delivery mechanism through dedicated personnel providing technical advisory, training, and problem-solving support tailored to science park users.
first_order_effect: Agricultural officers deployed to science parks; farmers at these sites receive free-of-cost technical guidance on modern farming methods, equipment use, and pest management.
second_order_effect: Knowledge diffusion to neighboring farmers; adoption of improved practices spreads beyond park users; farmer compliance with best practices increases, reducing crop failure rates.
third_order_effect: District-level agricultural productivity increases; demand for formal agricultural education rises; state capacity for extension services strengthens systemically.

impact_mechanism: Skill-building and research-to-farm-practice mechanism targeting youth, creating employment pathways while bridging research-farmer knowledge gap.
first_order_effect: Youth agricultural programs and research stations established; estimated ~5,000-15,000 youth enrolled in skills training annually; new research stations operational in 4-6 districts.
second_order_effect: Research outputs (improved seed varieties, organic protocols) reach farmers faster; youth employment in agriculture increases from baseline ~8% to ~12-15% of agricultural workforce within 3 years.
third_order_effect: Agricultural sector gains educated workforce; innovation adoption accelerates; climate-resilient crop varieties and techniques develop locally, reducing climate vulnerability.

promise_text_en: Introduce cost-effective agricultural technologies and equipment through our new agricultural policy

impact_mechanism: Price subsidy / access mechanism reducing upfront capital burden for smallholders to purchase or lease modern equipment, lowering per-unit production costs.
first_order_effect: Cost-effective technologies (drip irrigation kits, precision seeders, sprayers) made available at subsidized rates or through rental schemes; estimated reach ~3-8 lakh farmers.
second_order_effect: Smallholder farmers reduce input waste (water, fertilizer, seeds); per-hectare costs drop by ~20-30%; productivity increases on marginal holdings; marginal farmers' income floor rises.
third_order_effect: Aggregate agricultural output increases without major land expansion; water table stress reduces in water-scarce regions; crop diversity improves as viable alternatives become affordable.

impact_mechanism: Research infrastructure provision generating location-specific, climate-adaptive agricultural knowledge reducing crop failure and enabling sectoral diversification beyond cereals.
first_order_effect: Agricultural research centers established in crop, dairy, and allied sectors across TN; estimated 6-8 centers operational; staff recruited (~300-500 scientists/technicians).
second_order_effect: Research outputs (disease-resistant varieties, organic protocols, livestock breeds suited to TN climate) reach extension system; farmer adoption increases gradually; farm productivity rises by ~8-12% over 4-5 years.
third_order_effect: TN agriculture becomes increasingly climate-resilient and diversified; export-quality products developed; farmer incomes stabilize; state builds reputational advantage in agricultural innovation.

promise_text_en: Provide agricultural credit support to farmers through cooperative societies and agricultural development banks, with specific provisions for crop insurance and market linkages

impact_mechanism: Credit access + debt relief + market linkage mechanism simultaneously lowering borrowing barriers, reducing interest burden, and securing guaranteed offtake, stabilizing farmer incomes.
first_order_effect: Agricultural credit expanded through cooperatives and development banks with bundled crop insurance and market linkages; estimated reach ~15-25 lakh farmers; average credit per farmer ~₹1-2 lakh.
second_order_effect: Farmers reduce reliance on informal credit (moneylenders at 36-60% interest); formal debt servicing improves; risk of crop failure buffered by insurance; secured market linkages reduce price volatility exposure.
third_order_effect: Farmer default rates fall; cooperative strength improves; formal financial inclusion deepens; agricultural debt cycles weaken; smallholder agricultural persistence increases, reducing distress sales and debt-driven migration.

promise_text_en: Implement comprehensive agricultural reforms including soil health cards, crop insurance schemes, and farmer support programs across all agricultural districts

impact_mechanism: Multi-layered diagnostic + insurance + support mechanism addressing soil degradation (input efficiency), production risk (crop failure), and income volatility (targeted aid) simultaneously.
first_order_effect: Soil health cards issued to all farmers in TN (~25-30 lakh cards); crop insurance and farmer support programs (emergency aid, input subsidies) rolled out district-wide; estimated coverage ~2.5 crore farmer households.
second_order_effect: Farmers optimize fertilizer use based on soil data, reducing input costs by ~15-20%; insurance claims reduce farm distress; support programs stabilize incomes during failure years; rural household consumption volatility decreases.
third_order_effect: Agricultural sustainability improves (soil organic matter increases, runoff decreases); farmer household debt burden eases; off-farm income dependency reduces; rural poverty incidence falls by ~5-8 percentage points over 5 years.

promise_text_en: Provide agricultural support to 1,75,000 agricultural workers and extend coverage to 2,00,000 additional farmers in drought-affected regions

impact_mechanism: Direct income transfer / social protection mechanism providing cash support to landless and marginal farmers, raising income floor in vulnerable regions.
first_order_effect: Agricultural support (cash transfers / input subsidies / wages) provided to 1,75,000 agricultural workers + 2,00,000 drought-affected farmers (~3,75,000 total); estimated annual outlay ~₹300-500 cr (~₹80,000-1,33,000 per beneficiary per year).
second_order_effect: Vulnerable farm households' food security improves; children's school attendance increases (reduced child labor); consumption smoothing reduces distress asset sales; seasonal migration temporarily declines.
third_order_effect: Drought-affected regions stabilize; local economic activity sustains during dry seasons; human capital formation (education) improves in vulnerable households; long-term poverty persistence reduces but remains fragile without livelihood diversification.

impact_mechanism: Direct market access and reduced transaction costs through cooperative aggregation eliminates middleman margins and increases farm-gate prices for member farmers.
first_order_effect: ~8-12 lakh smallholder farmers join cooperatives/FPOs; average transaction cost per quintal falls by 15-25%; farmers capture 10-15% higher price realization on marketed surplus.
second_order_effect: Increased net farm income incentivizes intensification and crop diversification; improved cash flow reduces seasonal credit dependency on informal lenders at exploitative rates.
third_order_effect: Over 5 years, cooperative dividend reinvestment and retained earnings enable members to invest in farm mechanization and soil health, raising productivity and resilience to climate shocks.

impact_mechanism: Capital injection combined with skill development and buyer linkages converts SHG members from subsistence borrowers into productive entrepreneurs generating sustainable household income.
first_order_effect: ~25-30 lakh SHG members (primarily women HoH) receive average ₹50,000-₹1,00,000 credit + vocational training; ~40-50% move into income-generating micro-enterprises (food processing, textiles, handicrafts, retail).
second_order_effect: Participating households increase monthly income by ₹5,000-₹12,000; female financial autonomy rises; loan repayment rates remain high (SHG track record 95%+), freeing capital for next cohort.
third_order_effect: Multi-generational impact: improved household nutrition and child education outcomes; daughters' school enrollment and retention increase; intergenerational debt traps weaken.

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure provision (roads, water, electricity) reduces transaction costs, enables market access, and improves livelihood resilience in underserved rural/drought zones.
first_order_effect: ~3-4 crore rural residents in backward/drought-prone taluks gain reliable year-round water access and grid electricity; road connectivity extends to ~70% of habitations; travel time to town markets falls from 4-6 hrs to 1-2 hrs.
second_order_effect: Reduced water stress cuts agricultural losses in dry seasons by 20-30%; electrification enables cold storage and small processing units; market access attracts small agro-industries and retail networks to rural towns.
third_order_effect: Rural-urban wage gap narrows as productivity rises and non-farm employment emerges locally; out-migration for seasonal work declines; school and health facility usage improve due to access.

impact_mechanism: Government-certified skill training equips rural youth with market-relevant competencies enabling transition from agricultural wage labor to higher-wage non-farm employment.

second_order_effect: Youth earning growth reduces household dependence on volatile agricultural income; remittances from skilled youth fund family asset purchases and education; female youth participation in training rises (targeting +15-20% intake).
third_order_effect: Over time, skilled non-farm workforce attracts manufacturing and service-sector investment to secondary towns; local employment multiplier effect reduces pressure on metro migration.

impact_mechanism: Intensive, government-recognized digital certification for 45,000 unemployed youth directly bridges skills gap and enables job placement in tech-enabled enterprises and BPO/IT-enabled services.
first_order_effect: 45,000 unemployed youth (aged 18-35, majority likely rural/semi-urban) complete 3-6 month digital literacy + vocational modules; government certification issued; ~35-40% (15,750-18,000) placed in formal or gig employment at ₹10,000-₹15,000/month starting wages.
second_order_effect: Placed cohort begins contributing to household income and tax base; wage earnings fund further upskilling or entrepreneurship; unpaced youth create demand pressure for second/third training batches.
third_order_effect: Sustained annual cohort throughput (if replicated) gradually raises state's digital workforce stock; attracts back-office and data processing operations; fiscal returns via income tax exceed program cost by year 3-4.

impact_mechanism: E-governance digitization reduces bureaucratic delays, improves service transparency, and lowers administrative rent-seeking costs for citizens accessing government schemes and licenses.
first_order_effect: ~95% of state revenue department, land records, ration distribution, welfare application processing and license issuance migrate online; citizen wait times fall 70-80%; document forgery and discretionary denial cases drop 40-60%.
second_order_effect: Faster, transparent access to land titles, ration cards, and welfare benefits reduces compliance costs for poor households; informal payments and intermediary fees decline; tax compliance and scheme uptake improve.
third_order_effect: Over 5 years, improved public service efficiency builds institutional credibility; reduced corruption frees fiscal space; digital data analytics enable better targeting of schemes to actual poor.

impact_mechanism: Blockchain-based digital agricultural records and real-time crop monitoring enable precise, timely government support (insurance payouts, input subsidies, weather advisories) and reduce information asymmetry between farmer and market.
first_order_effect: ~2-3 crore farmer records digitized with geospatial crop data; ~60-70% of TN farmland under IoT/satellite monitoring; crop loss claims processed in 2-3 weeks vs. 6-9 months; farmers access price data and weather alerts via SMS/app.
second_order_effect: Faster, transparent claim settlement reduces farmer distress liquidation of assets; real-time crop advice cuts pest losses by 15-20% and improves yield by 8-12%; price transparency enables better planting decisions, reducing glut-driven income crashes.
third_order_effect: Improved predictability of farm income and faster liquidity reduce informal credit dependency; farmers invest more in soil health and diversification; blockchain traceability attracts premium-price buyers for branded/organic produce.

impact_mechanism: Free/subsidized laptops and materials remove upfront capital barrier to digital access for EWS students, enabling equal engagement with online learning, skill-building, and credential acquisition.

second_order_effect: EWS students access online tutoring, coding courses, and certification platforms; exam performance and college/job placement rates improve; girls' digital confidence and STEM enrollment increase (+8-12% projected).
third_order_effect: Cohort aging through labor market accumulates digital competencies, raising lifetime earning potential by 20-30%; intergenerational digital literacy transmission begins; reduces urban-rural skill gap.

impact_mechanism: Free distribution of textbooks and digital learning materials removes the financial and logistical barrier to curriculum access, enabling attendance and comprehension for students in households unable to purchase materials.
first_order_effect: ~1.5 crore school students and ~40 lakh college students gain assured access to prescribed textbooks and digital resources; eliminates recurring textbook purchase costs for BPL and lower-middle-income households.

impact_mechanism: Direct fee waivers and scholarships eliminate the upfront and recurring cost barrier to school/college enrollment, enabling disadvantaged students to attend and complete schooling.

third_order_effect: Higher intergenerational mobility as previously excluded cohorts complete tertiary education; expanded pool of skilled and semi-skilled labor; long-term wage premium for scholarship recipients reduces adult poverty persistence.

impact_mechanism: Targeted skill training and employment guidance for discriminated/marginalized students provides alternative income pathways and job-readiness, converting educational exclusion into workforce participation.
first_order_effect: Skill programs reach ~3–5 lakh students facing caste, gender, or disability barriers; ~40–60% of trainees secure formal or informal sector employment within 6 months of program completion.
second_order_effect: Excluded cohorts transition from zero or subsistence income to regular wages (₹8,000–15,000/month estimated); social and economic status rises within households and communities; reduced dependence on exploitative informal livelihoods.
third_order_effect: Sustained generational change as employed cohorts invest in children's education and health; normalization of non-traditional occupations for marginalized groups; modest sectoral reallocation toward skill-intensive industries.

impact_mechanism: SHG formation with skill training and livelihood support enables collectivization and income generation, converting informal unpaid work into collective productive asset and regular cash flow.
first_order_effect: ~2–3 lakh new/strengthened SHGs formed (typical target for large states); ~6–9 lakh women gain access to group credit, skill modules, and market linkages; average monthly income per SHG member rises ₹2,000–4,000.

third_order_effect: Long-term: SHGs mature into durable financial institutions, enabling women to access bank credit independently; cultural norms around women's work participation shift; female LFPR rises from current 33% urban baseline.

impact_mechanism: Bundled subsidized healthcare, maternity benefits, nutrition, sanitation, and education support reduces multiple simultaneous deficits (health, nutrition, education) that trap low-income families in poverty cycles.
first_order_effect: ~1.2–1.5 crore BPL women and families access subsidized health/maternity services; ~5–7 lakh maternity benefits disbursed (estimated); nutritional supplementation reaches ~80 lakh mothers and children; sanitation facility access expands in underserved blocks.
second_order_effect: Maternal mortality and child malnutrition rates decline; school enrollment among aided children's cohorts rises; out-of-pocket health spending falls, freeing household income for food/assets; women's health literacy and agency improve through clinic interactions.
third_order_effect: Over 10 years, cohorts with early nutritional and health investment show better school performance and lifelong earnings; generational reduction in stunting/undernutrition; lower fertility rates as women gain education and autonomy.

impact_mechanism: Fair pricing mechanisms, credit facilities, and market access remove the cost and information barriers that force farmers into distress sales, enabling capture of full commodity value and investment in improved practices.
first_order_effect: ~10–15 lakh farming households gain improved market linkages and fair-price guarantees; access to agricultural credit expands; farm-gate prices rise 10–15% for supported crops; debt burden among aided farmers stabilizes or declines.
second_order_effect: Increased reinvestment in soil health, seeds, and tools; adoption of improved varieties and practices accelerates; farm incomes stabilize, reducing seasonal income volatility and distress borrowing; farmer suicides plateau or decline.
third_order_effect: Over 8–10 years, sustained productivity gains and reduced rural indebtedness enable transition to higher-value crops and diversified enterprises; rural non-farm income opportunities expand; agricultural wages and rural LFPR shift upward.

impact_mechanism: Housing improvement and water access provision removes the health, dignity, and security deficits of inadequate shelter and contaminated water, enabling improved health and household stability.
first_order_effect: ~15–25 lakh rural households gain new/improved housing; ~20–30 lakh households gain piped drinking water access; waterborne disease incidence falls in aided blocks; child and adult health outcomes improve measurably.
second_order_effect: School attendance rises as health burden and shame-related absenteeism fall; labor productivity increases; women's safety and time-burden (water collection) improve; property-linked social status and credit access expand for beneficiary households.
third_order_effect: Long-term: rural health baseline strengthens; human capital accumulation accelerates; rural-urban migration pressures moderate as living conditions improve; gender inequality in care work and water access declines.

impact_mechanism: Reliable electricity and water management infrastructure removes production and livelihood constraints, enabling agricultural productivity, small enterprise viability, and quality-of-life gains in rural communities.
first_order_effect: ~25,000–35,000 villages gain 24/7 electricity supply; water management (tanki, bore, canal) coverage expands to underserved areas; irrigation hours and reliability increase; rural small business and processing feasibility improves.
second_order_effect: Agricultural yields rise 15–25% where irrigation reliability improves; non-farm enterprises (cold storage, processing, retail) become viable; rural employment diversification accelerates; youth out-migration pressure moderates.
third_order_effect: Over 10–15 years, electrified villages develop as centers for agro-processing and light manufacturing; rural income diversification reduces agricultural dependence; gender-differentiated labor opportunities expand, lifting female LFPR.

impact_mechanism: Institutional consolidation of fragmented disability services under a single department creates clarity of authority and accountability, enabling coordinated access to support for families currently navigating multiple unconnected agencies.
first_order_effect: A dedicated department is established with administrative capacity to inventory existing services, identify gaps in autism/cerebral palsy/developmental disorder support, and create referral pathways; estimated 8–12 lakh persons with non-conventional disabilities in TN gain a single point of contact.
second_order_effect: Early identification and enrollment in support services increases as awareness spreads and administrative friction decreases; school enrollment rates and therapy uptake among eligible children rise as families encounter fewer bureaucratic barriers.
third_order_effect: Over 5–10 years, reduced undiagnosed disability prevalence improves mainstream educational outcomes and labor force entry for this cohort, reducing long-term dependency and caregiver burden.

promise_text_en: Establish support services and specialized programs for children diagnosed with autism, including speech therapy, occupational therapy, and educational interventions

impact_mechanism: Provision of specialized therapeutic and educational services (speech, occupational therapy, educational interventions) directly addresses the developmental gaps in autistic children by creating structured access to evidence-based early intervention.
first_order_effect: Specialized programs operationalized across urban centers and district headquarters; estimated 1.5–2 lakh children with autism diagnosis in TN gain access to coordinated speech, occupational, and educational therapy within a 5-year rollout.
second_order_effect: Early therapeutic intervention improves language, motor, and social skills in children aged 3–8, increasing ability to integrate into mainstream or specialized schools and reducing need for institutional care.
third_order_effect: Children who receive early intervention show improved educational trajectories and social functioning into adulthood; family caregiver stress reduces, freeing parental labor for workforce participation.

impact_mechanism: Removal of transport and accessibility barriers through dedicated support services enables children with disabilities to physically access educational institutions, converting enrollment eligibility into actual attendance.
first_order_effect: Special school transport services and accessibility retrofits (ramps, toilets, mobility aids) deployed to ~500–1,000 schools and colleges; estimated 5–8 lakh children with mobility, sensory, or severe physical disabilities gain reliable access to educational sites.
second_order_effect: Attendance rates among enrolled disabled children increase sharply (baseline ~40–50% to potential 75–85%); peer integration and mainstream curriculum exposure improve, reducing stigma and institutional segregation.
third_order_effect: Over 5–10 years, disabled cohorts complete higher education at rates approaching non-disabled peers; employers encounter normalized workplace diversity, reducing hiring discrimination and expanding employment pathways.

impact_mechanism: A branded, dedicated scheme for learning disabilities (dyslexia, dyscalculia, ADHD) creates institutional visibility and dedicated budgetary allocation, enabling systematic identification and targeted remediation that standard schooling does not provide.
first_order_effect: 'Uyarkkali Mozhi' program launched with screening, diagnosis, and remedial coaching infrastructure; estimated 8–12 lakh children in TN with undiagnosed learning disabilities are identified and enrolled in differentiated instruction within 5 years.
second_order_effect: Individualized learning interventions reduce failure rates and class repetition; affected children progress through primary school on grade-appropriate timelines, improving confidence and reducing dropout risk during middle/secondary transition.
third_order_effect: Cohorts completing secondary education with literacy and numeracy competency enable access to vocational training and skilled employment; intergenerational educational mobility increases as children avoid the poverty–illiteracy trap.

promise_text_en: Provide special education support and remedial coaching for children (ages 4-12) with learning disabilities such as dyslexia, dyscalculia, and ADHD

impact_mechanism: Direct provision of specialized remedial coaching addresses the cognitive skill gaps in children with learning disabilities through age-targeted, evidence-based interventions, enabling grade-appropriate progression.
first_order_effect: Remedial coaching programs (special educators, assistive technology, multisensory methods) deployed to reach ~6–10 lakh children aged 4–12 with dyslexia, dyscalculia, or ADHD; coaching delivered in schools or community centers.
second_order_effect: Children receiving targeted coaching show measurable gains in literacy and numeracy; retention in grade improves from typical 20–30% repetition rates toward single-digit rates; intrinsic motivation to continue schooling increases.
third_order_effect: Longitudinal progression into secondary school enables skill-building in vocational domains; reduced school dropout translates to larger cohorts entering apprenticeships and lower-skill employment, raising household incomes.

promise_text_en: Ensure that schools recognize and support children with learning disabilities by providing individualized education plans and necessary accommodations

impact_mechanism: Mandated individualized education plans (IEPs) and school-level accommodations create legally binding accountability for recognizing and supporting learning-disabled children within mainstream schools, shifting from exclusion to inclusion.
first_order_effect: Schools mandated to conduct learning disability screening, develop IEPs, and provide accommodations (extra time, assistive tech, classroom modification); estimated 10–15 lakh children in mainstream schools benefit from formal recognition and individualized supports.
second_order_effect: Reduced stigma as IEPs normalize support; disabled children remain in mainstream schools instead of being shunted to separate streams; peer relationships and social inclusion improve, reducing psychological distress.
third_order_effect: Mainstreamed cohorts develop stronger peer networks and social capital; graduation rates improve, enabling transition to competitive employment or further education with reduced shame or identity fragmentation.

promise_text_en: Develop infrastructure and community living facilities for disabled persons with emphasis on independent and supported community-based living

impact_mechanism: Development of community-based living facilities provides disabled persons with structural alternatives to institutional warehousing or full family dependency, enabling dignified, supported autonomy.
first_order_effect: Community living facilities (group homes, day centers, supported apartments) developed in urban and semi-urban areas; estimated 5–8 lakh disabled persons gain access to independent or semi-independent living options within 5–7 years.
second_order_effect: Disabled persons transition out of custodial institutions or total family dependency; they develop daily living skills, social networks, and potential for part-time employment or income-generating activities.
third_order_effect: Over 10–15 years, normalized community integration reduces stigma and increases employer familiarity with disabled workers; employment rates and income among working-age disabled persons rise; caregiver burden on aging families decreases.

promise_text_en: Introduce a national employment registry for persons with disabilities (C.1 Schedule) to facilitate job placements and livelihood opportunities

impact_mechanism: A centralized employment registry matching disabled persons with available job vacancies reduces information asymmetry and employer discrimination, enabling labor market access for an underutilized workforce.
first_order_effect: Employment registry built and operationalized; estimated 15–25 lakh persons with disabilities (Schedule C.1) registered; employers notified of available candidates; ~2–5% of registered candidates placed in jobs within 3–5 years (conservative estimate given baseline low disability employment rates of 0.5–1%).
second_order_effect: Regular employment income for placed individuals reduces household poverty and caregiver dependence; working disabled persons develop workplace confidence and professional networks, increasing retention and advancement.
third_order_effect: Expanding cohorts of employed disabled persons normalize workplace diversity; employer practices shift to accommodate disabled workers, reducing discrimination and opening pathways for secondary/tertiary placements even among non-registry candidates.

promise_text_en: Establish 'Matrutvam' scheme providing elderly women with comprehensive healthcare including maternal benefits, nutrition support, disability allowance, insurance coverage, and 24/7 medical assistance

impact_mechanism: Income transfer combined with access to healthcare and nutrition support removes three simultaneous financial and health barriers faced by elderly women living below or near poverty line.
first_order_effect: Elderly women (estimated ~1.5–2.0 crore in TN, concentrated in BPL households) gain access to subsidized/free medication, nutrition rations, disability/old-age allowance (~₹1,000–2,000/month assumed), and insurance; 24/7helpline operational.
second_order_effect: Reduced catastrophic health expenditure and postponement of medical crises allow elderly women to avoid asset-selling and debt; improved nutrition compliance reduces hospitalization frequency and enables functional independence longer.
third_order_effect: Over 5+ years, lower morbidity in elderly cohort reduces demand pressure on tertiary care; female caregivers (typically daughters-in-law) freed for workforce participation, increasing household labor supply and consumption smoothing.

promise_text_en: Under Matrutvam scheme, provide elderly women with comprehensive services including medication support, nutrition (food items), disability/old age allowance, insurance coverage, and emergency medical assistance

impact_mechanism: Bundled provision of medication, nutrition, disability allowance, insurance, and emergency support addresses the multi-dimensional poverty trap affecting elderly women by reducing out-of-pocket health spending.
first_order_effect: ~1.5–2.0 crore elderly women in TN receive medication subsidies, food rations (estimated ₹2,000–4,000/month in kind), old-age/disability allowance, health insurance, and 24/7 medical hotline access.
second_order_effect: Improved medication adherence and nutritional status lower acute disease episodes; insurance coverage shifts financial risk away from household, enabling savings accumulation instead of crisis borrowing.
third_order_effect: Sustained health improvement reduces elderly dependency on informal caregiving, improving caregiver productivity; intergenerational health gains if better-nourished grandmothers improve early-childhood nutrition outcomes.

promise_text_en: Establish specialized geriatric care (MD Geriatric Care) programs as dedicated medical departments for elderly women's healthcare

impact_mechanism: Specialist geriatric medicine training and dedicated departments create localized clinical capacity to diagnose and manage age-specific conditions (polypharmacy, cognitive decline, falls, chronic comorbidities) that primary care systems typically miss.
first_order_effect: MD Geriatric Care posts established at tertiary medical colleges in TN (estimated 2–4 departments state-wide); first cohort of 10–20 geriatricians trained over 3 years; referral pathway created from PHC/CHC to specialist care.
second_order_effect: Earlier diagnosis of reversible conditions (e.g., depression, nutritional deficiency) and optimized polypharmacy reduce preventable hospitalizations; improved clinical protocols cascade to primary care through CME and guideline dissemination.
third_order_effect: Over 5+ years, geriatric care quality benchmarks established; improved outcomes data attracts federal funding and clinical fellowships; geriatric competency becomes standard in medical curriculum, building sustainable workforce.

promise_text_en: Provide 24-hour emergency medical assistance and healthcare support for elderly women through Matrutvam scheme

impact_mechanism: 24-hour emergency helpline and rapid-response medical assistance removes the critical access barrier that delays treatment in acute episodes (stroke, MI, fall injury) common in elderly women living alone or in resource-poor settings.
first_order_effect: Toll-free 24/7 medical helpline launched and staffed; ambulance dispatch network or medical-team response (response time target <30 min in urban, <60 min rural) operational for elderly women enrolled in Matrutvam.
second_order_effect: Reduced time-to-treatment in acute episodes (stroke, sepsis, trauma) improves survival rates and functional recovery; psychological security from knowing help is accessible reduces health-anxiety and non-adherence.
third_order_effect: Aggregate mortality and disability-adjusted life-years (DALYs) decline in elderly female cohort; data on call patterns reveals unmet primary care needs, informing preventive program redesign.

promise_text_en: Strengthen support systems and social security measures for elderly women including institutional care and dignified living arrangements

impact_mechanism: Institutional care and dignified living arrangements address the structural vulnerability of elderly women (widowed, childless, abandoned, or in poor-quality informal shelter) by providing secure, health-monitored residential alternatives.
first_order_effect: Old-age homes and supported-living facilities expanded or newly established (estimated capacity increase 5,000–10,000 beds); admission criteria prioritize homeless, abandoned, or severely disabled elderly women; subsidized beds scale.
second_order_effect: Beneficiaries access regular meals, hygiene, nursing oversight, and social engagement; reduced falls and infections from improved environmental safety; family caregivers in other households relieved, allowing remarriage/remarriage of widows or workforce re-entry.
third_order_effect: Community perception of institutionalization shifts from stigma to viable care option; long-term cost per capita (institutional) vs. community-based care becomes transparent, informing policy portfolio allocation.

promise_text_en: Address elderly women's healthcare needs and vulnerabilities through comprehensive social welfare and security programs

impact_mechanism: Broad social welfare and security programs addressing healthcare, livelihood, and institutional support simultaneously target the intersecting vulnerabilities (health, poverty, social isolation, gender discrimination) that elderly women face.
first_order_effect: Portfolio of programs (medical care, nutrition, allowances, institutional care, legal aid, community outreach) implemented in phased manner; beneficiary enrollment targets ~1.5–2.0 crore elderly women across BPL and vulnerable APL cohorts.
second_order_effect: Multi-dimensional poverty indicators (health, income, access, security) decline simultaneously; reduced emergency-care demand at hospitals frees budget for preventive services; women's household decision-making power improves with income security.
third_order_effect: Elderly women's economic participation and social mobility increase; gender norms around elder care and widow status shift as institutional support legitimizes alternatives to patriarchal family structures.

promise_text_en: Ban harmful pesticides including Ammonium Nitrate, Monocrotophos, Chlorpyriphos, Acephate, and Endosulfan in Tamil Nadu and replace them with organic alternatives

impact_mechanism: Pesticide ban reduces direct chemical exposure (skin, respiratory, neurological toxicity) in farmer populations and eliminates bioaccumulation in food chains, protecting both agricultural workers and consumers.
first_order_effect: Five hazardous pesticide brands phased out or banned over 24–36 months; ~10–15 lakh farmer households (primary users) transition to organic/less-toxic alternatives; residue levels in foodgrains decline measurably in post-harvest testing.
second_order_effect: Acute poisoning cases (unintentional ingestion, spray exposure) in agro-populations decline; long-term cancer and neurological disease incidence declines after 10+ year latency; farmer healthcare costs (occupational illness) fall.
third_order_effect: Consumer trust in TN agricultural products increases due to lower pesticide residues; organic premium markets develop; demand for certification and training in organic methods rises, creating new rural employment and shifting commodity value chains.

promise_text_en: Provide fair pricing to farmers for their produce. Ensure farmers receive support prices and establish mechanisms to protect farmer income
promise_text_ta: àöõ˜èÀ‚ªèù îQò£è õ£Kò‹ Ü¬ñ‚èŠð´‹. Üõ˜èO¡ C‚è™èœ b˜‚èŠð´‹. àöõ˜èœ M¬÷M‚°‹ ªð£¼œèÀ‚° àöõ˜è¬÷‚ ªè£‡´ Ü¬ñ‚èŠð†ì ïôõ£Kò«ñ ÜõŸPŸè£ù M¬ô¬ò º®¾ ªêŒ»‹
impact_mechanism: Support prices and income-protection mechanisms provide a price floor that prevents farmers from selling below production cost, protecting household income stability and reducing distress-sale behavior during market gluts.
first_order_effect: Minimum support price (MSP) or state procurement price set above cost-of-cultivation for major crops (paddy, sugarcane, cotton, groundnut, etc.); procurement agency (FCI, state cooperatives) guarantees purchase at announced price; ~8–10 lakh farmer households benefit with stabilized revenue.
second_order_effect: Farmers reduce fire-sales during surplus seasons, improving income predictability; debt repayment regularity improves, reducing formal/informal borrowing; fertilizer/seed quality investment increases as income risk falls.
third_order_effect: Over 3–5 years, agricultural productivity investment (land, equipment, soil testing) rises as income security enables long-term planning; farmer suicide rates decline in supported crop clusters; cooperative strength increases as market linkage stabilizes.

promise_text_en: Achieve 100% organic farming certification in Tamil Nadu, following the model of countries like Bhutan, Australia, and Switzerland
promise_text_ta: Bhutan, Australia, Switzerland «ð£¡ø ï£´èÀ‚°„ ªê¡Á CøŠ¹Š ðJŸC ªðø Üó² ê£˜H™ ãŸð£´ ªêŒòŠð´‹. Þ‰Fò£M™ C‚A‹ ñ£Gô‹ àôA«ô«ò 100% ÞòŸ¬è «õ÷£‡¬ñ ªêŒ»‹
impact_mechanism: Mandating 100% organic certification eliminates synthetic pesticide/fertilizer use, reducing input costs and soil degradation while creating premium-price market access for certified farmers.
first_order_effect: All ~26 lakh TN farming households transition to organic methods; certification infrastructure (labs, auditors, testing) must be scaled from ~5% current coverage to universal coverage within 5 years.
second_order_effect: Transition-phase yield decline (typically 20–40% over 2–3 years) reduces farm income before soil recovery; simultaneously, organic input supply chains collapse if conventional inputs are legally prohibited, forcing farmers into unregulated/black markets or abandonment of land.
third_order_effect: Land consolidation accelerates as marginal/small farmers (60% of TN) exit farming; agrarian distress worsens unless simultaneous income support, market guarantees, and certified organic buyer networks are operational—without these, rural unemployment and distress migration spike.

promise_text_en: Establish organic farming certification schemes for farmers and provide support to transition to organic farming practices

impact_mechanism: Certification schemes + transition support (subsidy, training, inputs) reduce the financial and knowledge barriers preventing voluntary conversion to organic farming, enabling market-driven adoption.
first_order_effect: ~3–5 lakh farmers adopt organic methods over 5 years with government support; certification bodies established; transition subsidies (₹500–1,000/acre/year typical) disbursed to early adopters.
second_order_effect: Certified organic output creates differentiated market segments; farm-gate prices rise 15–25% for certified produce, but only if buyer networks and export/retail channels actively procure; simultaneous soil health improves (lower pest/disease pressure, reduced agrochemical runoff).
third_order_effect: Sustainable intensification pathway emerges: soil carbon buildup, water retention, biodiversity recovery enable stable yields at lower input cost by year 4–5; farmer income stabilizes above baseline if markets absorb certified supply.

promise_text_en: Provide financial assistance and study support to farmers' children pursuing education, ensuring equal opportunities

impact_mechanism: Direct income support to farmer families via education stipends/scholarships lowers opportunity cost of schooling and removes debt-driven dropouts, enabling human capital accumulation in agrarian households.
first_order_effect: ~1.5–2 lakh farmers' children (assuming ~7–8% of 26 lakh farming families with school-age kids) receive scholarships; secondary/tertiary enrollment from farming backgrounds increases by 10–15% if scheme is well-publicized.
second_order_effect: Educational attainment in rural/agrarian cohorts rises; youth transition into non-farm employment (services, IT, skilled trades) or return to farming with modern knowledge; rural outmigration temporarily accelerates but with higher human capital.
third_order_effect: Intergenerational escape from agrarian poverty becomes viable; rural wage labor quality improves; agricultural innovation adoption (new varieties, machinery, digital tools) accelerates as educated youth re-engage farming or transmit knowledge; farming becomes less stigmatized.

promise_text_en: Support traditional farming practices and provide assistance for natural/indigenous cultivation methods

impact_mechanism: Subsidizing indigenous/traditional cultivation methods (millets, pulses, intercropping, water harvesting) restores low-input, climate-adaptive farming practices that reduce input debt and water stress.
first_order_effect: ~2–4 lakh farmers adopt traditional practices with government support; millet and pulse acreage expands by 10–15% in target districts; water table pressure eases in groundwater-stressed regions (Madurai, Salem, Tiruppur belts).
second_order_effect: Nutritional intake improves in farming households (millets/pulses nutrient-dense); input costs fall 30–40% (no synthetic fertilizer/pesticide bills); farm profitability rises if output prices remain stable or rise.
third_order_effect: Climate resilience strengthens: lower water demand, improved soil structure, diversified cropping reduce vulnerability to drought; rural incomes stabilize even in deficit rainfall years; market demand for pulses/millets grows as health awareness rises.

promise_text_en: Ensure crop-specific training and knowledge transfer programs for farmers, with emphasis on sustainable practices and soil health

impact_mechanism: Crop-specific training transfers soil health and sustainable practice knowledge, enabling farmers to optimize yields, reduce input waste, and build agronomic capacity for long-term productivity.
first_order_effect: ~5–8 lakh farmers receive training (via KVK, extension agents, farmer clusters) over 5 years; soil health cards issued; crop-specific advisory adopted by ~30–40% of trained cohort; yields in trained cohorts improve 8–15% within 2 years.
second_order_effect: Input use efficiency increases (less fertilizer/water per unit output); production cost per hectare falls 10–20%; soil organic matter accumulates; water retention improves, reducing irrigation frequency and groundwater draft.
third_order_effect: Farm productivity baseline shifts upward; smaller landholdings (< 1 hectare) become viable at subsistence+ incomes; groundwater depletion rate slows; natural pest/disease suppressants improve crop health, reducing chemical spraying and associated health/environmental costs.

promise_text_en: Establish 14 government schools in the curriculum framework. Establish 608 educational institutions. 12 months of free education for government school students. Implement 12-month free education for government school students.
promise_text_ta: Iè c‡ì èìŸè¬ó¬ò‚ ªè£‡ì îI›ï£†®™ 14 èì«ô£ó ñ£õ†ìƒèœ Þ¼‚A¡øù. 608 eùõ CŸÚ˜èœ Þ¼‚A¡øù. êŸªø£Šð 12 Þô‚èˆFŸ°  «ñô£ù eùõ ñ‚èœ
impact_mechanism: Establishing 14 new schools + 608 institutions with 12-month free education removes user fees and expands school access, eliminating direct cost barriers to enrollment and completion.
first_order_effect: 622 new educational institutions built/operationalized; ~100,000–150,000 additional students gain access to government schooling; fee abolition reduces annual household education cost by ₹2,000–5,000/child/year for ~4–5 crore government school students.
second_order_effect: Net primary/secondary enrollment rises 5–10%; dropout rates (currently 15–25% rural) fall by 8–12 percentage points; BPL household education spending reallocates to nutrition/health; teaching load per school increases without proportionate teacher recruitment, risking quality decline.
third_order_effect: Educational attainment floor rises in next generation; rural female LFPR (currently 51%) improves as girls remain in school longer; wage earning capacity in rural labor markets increases; agrarian distress pressures ease as economic mobility improves.

promise_text_en: Free education will be provided to students in government schools for 12 months.

impact_mechanism: Free education (12-month provision, likely meaning removal of fees/charges) directly reduces household cost of schooling, removing the primary financial barrier to enrollment and attendance.
first_order_effect: All ~4–5 crore government school students experience zero tuition/exam/material fees; household education cost falls ₹2,000–5,000/child/year; enrollment (particularly in secondary) rises 5–10% as opportunity cost to family falls.
second_order_effect: School attendance improves; dropout-driven income loss to poor households decreases; children remain in school longer; teaching quality depends on teacher training/resources (not solved by fee abolition alone), creating bifurcation risk: access ↑ but learning ↓ if not paired with teacher investment.
third_order_effect: Human capital accumulation accelerates for BPL/EWS cohorts; rural-urban educational gap narrows; intergenerational mobility improves; wage premium for educated labor tightens if job creation doesn't match skill supply.

promise_text_en: Establish a Chief Minister's Teacher Training Model to provide teacher training and quality education to government school students.

impact_mechanism: Systematic teacher training (via CM model) raises pedagogical quality, enabling better student learning outcomes, reduced dropout due to engagement, and more effective curriculum delivery.
first_order_effect: ~50,000–100,000 government teachers trained in new pedagogical methods over 5 years; training infrastructure (centers, master trainers) established; teaching methods shift from rote to competency-based; student learning gains (measured by standardized assessments) improve 10–20% in trained cohorts.
second_order_effect: School quality reputation improves; private school enrollment diversion to government schools accelerates (cost + quality competition); student engagement rises, reducing behavioral dropouts; teacher morale/retention improves if training linked to career progression.
third_order_effect: Government school system becomes sustainable alternative to private schooling; educational inequality (rich/poor, urban/rural) narrows; skilled labor supply to TN economy improves; state fiscal burden from education shifts from access (fee abolition) to quality (teacher investment).

promise_text_en: Supply school uniforms and mid-day meals to government school students.

impact_mechanism: Nutritional supplementation and removal of indirect schooling costs reduce household burden and improve school attendance among economically vulnerable families.
first_order_effect: Approximately 1.6 crore government school students receive daily meals and uniforms; household food expenditure pressure eases for ~2 crore ration-card families with school-age children.
second_order_effect: Improved nutritional intake increases cognitive function and classroom concentration; reduced dropout due to cost/stigma barriers allows grade progression and learning continuity.
third_order_effect: Over 5 years, cohorts completing secondary education with better foundational skills improve employability and wage potential, incrementally shifting intergenerational poverty dynamics.

promise_text_en: Distribute uniforms and mid-day meals free of cost to government school students. Support will also be extended to students from private schools and other educational institutions.

impact_mechanism: Extension of meal and uniform support to private school students reduces equity disparity and improves nutrition access across socioeconomic strata.
first_order_effect: Approximately 2.5–3.0 crore students (govt + private + other institutions) receive free uniforms and meals; fiscal cost rises ~40–50% vs. govt schools alone.
second_order_effect: Private school participation by lower-income families increases due to cost reduction; public-private education stratification narrows, but state subsidy burden expands significantly.
third_order_effect: Longer-term equity in learning outcomes across school types may emerge, but sustained fiscal pressure risks means-testing reversion or service quality degradation.

promise_text_en: Implement a comprehensive scheme involving 48,000 trained teachers, 6,000 teaching assistants, and 3 major teacher training institutes across 363 villages in government schools. Additionally, 6,800 anganwadi workers will be provided employment opportunities, and an innovative school meal scheme will be implemented for government school students.

amount_mentioned: 48,000 teachers, 6,000 assistants, 3 institutes, 363 villages, 6,800 anganwadi workers
impact_mechanism: Structural expansion of teaching workforce, teacher training, and anganwadi employment simultaneous to meal provision addresses both human capital supply and nutritional demand holistically.
first_order_effect: 48,000 teachers and 6,000 assistants hired; 6,800 anganwadi workers employed; 363 villages receive coordinated school meal infrastructure; estimated ~5–8 lakh direct beneficiary students in target villages plus anganwadi coverage.
second_order_effect: Reduced pupil-teacher ratios and structured training improve classroom pedagogy; anganwadi employment stabilizes women's income; meal production creates local supply chains and MGNREGA substitution.
third_order_effect: Village-level education and nutrition ecosystems mature; women's workforce participation increases modestly (~5–8% in target villages); learning outcomes measurably improve if training quality maintained.

promise_text_en: Establish an 'Education First' policy to protect and nurture government school students, with support for mid-day meals and school uniforms, and additional provisions to support students from all schools.

impact_mechanism: Policy-level protection framework for government schools, combined with meal/uniform provision and multi-school support, creates institutional accountability and cross-sector nutrition access.
first_order_effect: Policy codifies meal and uniform commitments; ~1.6–2.5 crore students across school types benefit; government schools receive prioritized resource allocation signaling.
second_order_effect: Government school enrollment stabilization and private school cost reduction lower absolute dropout rates; public-private participation becomes cost-neutral at household level.
third_order_effect: Long-term school system consolidation around equity outcomes; if policy survives budget cycles, intergenerational learning inequality begins to narrow.

impact_mechanism: Rural road construction reduces transport time and cost to markets, schools, and health facilities, enabling agricultural commercialization and service access for dispersed populations.
first_order_effect: 1,076 km of new rural roads constructed; estimated ~15–25 lakh people in underserved villages gain year-round road connectivity; ~₹800–1,200 cr capital cost over 3–5 years.
second_order_effect: Agricultural produce transport time/cost declines 30–50%; farm-to-market margins improve; school and health facility utilization rises; local labor demand increases during construction.
third_order_effect: Rural income from agricultural sales and services grows 8–15% cumulatively; migration pressure eases incrementally; village economies become less subsistence-dependent.

promise_text_en: Provide free electricity to all government schools and educational institutions

impact_mechanism: Elimination of electricity cost burden on school operations frees state per-student spending for pedagogy and infrastructure; improves service hours and facility quality (lighting, cooling, IT access).
first_order_effect: Free electricity supplied to ~30,000–35,000 government schools and educational institutions; annual operational savings ~₹400–600 cr redirect to other education expenditure.
second_order_effect: Schools extend operating hours and classroom lighting; computer labs and digital learning infrastructure viable; female student safety (lighting) improves; teacher resource allocation shifts from utilities to instruction.
third_order_effect: Digital and laboratory learning access normalizes across government schools; learning outcomes in STEM improve incrementally; rural education quality convergence with urban baseline begins.

promise_text_en: Ensure safe drinking water and improved water supply to rural areas through strengthened water management systems

first_order_effect: Upgraded water supply infrastructure reaches ~3–5 crore rural residents; disease incidence for diarrhea/typhoid declines ~20–35%; household water collection time reduces ~2–3 hours/day for beneficiary families.
second_order_effect: Child malnutrition severity decreases as infection burden falls; girls' school attendance improves (time freed from collection); adult labor productivity rises; health expenditure burden on poorest households eases.
third_order_effect: Over 10 years, cohorts with early water security show improved nutritional and cognitive baselines; female LFPR in rural areas incrementally rises 2–5% as collection time diminishes; preventable disease mortality converges toward state average.

second_order_effect: Women's borrowing costs decline vs. moneylenders (interest 20–50% reduction); informal savings discipline strengthens; income-generation activities (small manufacturing, services) scale; household debt-to-income ratios improve.
third_order_effect: Women's economic autonomy and intra-household bargaining power increase; female labor force participation rises 5–12% in SHG-active villages; intergenerational school enrollment for girls improves as household disposable income grows.

second_order_effect: Sustained school engagement reduces dropout rates and enables completion of foundational learning; household food expenditure is reallocated to other necessities.
third_order_effect: Higher literacy cohorts enter labor market with better cognitive development; intergenerational poverty transmission weakens as education attainment rises.

second_order_effect: Reduced migration pressure as skill-building and remote employment opportunities become available locally; agricultural information access improves pricing decisions.
third_order_effect: Rural wage growth accelerates; agricultural productivity gains from better information; long-term digital dividend enables higher-skilled rural workforce.

first_order_effect: ~8–10 lakh marginal and small farmers access government credit lines at lower rates; input acquisition accelerates; debt-trap cycles with informal lenders reduce.
second_order_effect: Input adoption rates rise (seeds, fertilizers); crop yields improve; farm household incomes increase; repayment capacity strengthens.
third_order_effect: Agricultural growth becomes self-reinforcing; credit-enabled farmers graduate to higher-value crops; rural wage employment expands in input supply chain.

third_order_effect: Reduced distress sales and seasonal debt; agricultural profit margins increase; farmers shift from subsistence to commercial farming orientation.

scheme_name: Guaranteed Minimum Price for All Farmers
impact_mechanism: Minimum price guarantee removes downside income risk for all farmers by committing state to purchase at predetermined floor prices, creating income security independent of market cycles.
first_order_effect: ~8–10 lakh farmers eligible for guaranteed pricing; government becomes buyer of last resort; budget commitment of ₹10,000–15,000 cr annually (estimated) competes with existing commitments.
second_order_effect: Farm income floor is established; cultivation decisions are decoupled from spot prices; overproduction risk emerges as all farmers respond to guaranteed prices simultaneously.
third_order_effect: Market gluts and storage costs balloon; procurement infrastructure (warehouses, logistics) becomes critical bottleneck; fiscal sustainability threatened if prices remain above cost.

second_order_effect: Productivity gains from better inputs; income volatility from weather shocks is partially absorbed; farmer investment in land conservation increases.
third_order_effect: Soil health and water retention improve; long-term yield trajectory accelerates; input use becomes more intensive, requiring complementary water/soil management programs.
