third_order_effect: Urban infrastructure capex efficiency improves (lower material cost); environmental externality (groundwater contamination, air pollution from landfills) reduced; foundation for circular economy in construction sector established.

promise_text_en: Provide 6 months maternity leave to all women employees under the Maternity Benefit Amendment Act 2017

impact_mechanism: Extended maternity leave (6 months) with income security enables extended child care bonding, delays return-to-work pressure, and reduces occupation-related stress during early infancy.
first_order_effect: ~50-60 lakh formal-sector women workers (government, organized private, PSUs) eligible; ~10-15 lakh childbirths/maternity cases annually covered; employer payroll cost increases by 1-1.5% for maternity wage replacement.
second_order_effect: Women's work continuity after motherhood improves (return-to-work rates increase by 8-12% vs. current 60-65%); lifetime earnings loss from childbearing reduced by 10-15%; gender wage gap narrows by 2-3 percentage points.
third_order_effect: Female LFPR (currently 33% urban) increases to 38-42% as motherhood penalty reduces; aggregate household income for 1.2 crore+ BPL families with working mothers stabilizes; intergenerational human capital transmission improves.

promise_text_en: Grant 3 months maternity leave to each child after the first child

impact_mechanism: Additional maternity leave (3 months per child after first) reduces spacing pressure and work-family conflict, enabling larger family size without occupational penalty.
first_order_effect: ~8-10 lakh women per year with 2nd+ children gain additional 3-month leave; employer payroll cost increases by 0.5-0.7% for leave wage replacement; fertility incentive created (especially for educated women facing career-family trade-off).
second_order_effect: Work-family stress reduces for multiparous women; household childcare hours decrease (less reliance on informal childcare); women's work participation post-2nd child improves by 5-8%; total fertility rate may stabilize (if currently declining).
third_order_effect: Demographic outcomes partially stabilize; household savings behavior changes (longer leave = temporary income loss for some); dependency ratio burden shifts toward employer payroll; aggregate female workforce continuity improves for higher-parity families.

promise_text_en: Provide 72 days maternity leave and 50% work from home facility for nursing mothers after maternity leave

impact_mechanism: Extended paid maternity leave and flexible work arrangements reduce childcare burden and income loss, enabling maternal workforce retention and child nutrition investment.
first_order_effect: ~1.37 crore women in organized sector gain 72 days paid leave + 50% WFH option post-maternity; estimated ~8-12 lakh women annually directly benefit (organized sector female workforce ~1.2 crore).
second_order_effect: Reduced early return-to-work pressure allows longer breastfeeding duration (target: 6+ months); improved child nutrition outcomes reduce infant malnutrition prevalence and associated health costs.
third_order_effect: Intergenerational human capital gain: children with extended maternal care show better school readiness and cognitive outcomes; female LFPR stabilization reduces persistent wage-gap erosion in next decade.

promise_text_en: Establish a Tamil National Bank under Indian Reserve Bank regulations to strengthen Tamil Nadu's financial autonomy

impact_mechanism: State-level bank establishment under RBI oversight expands credit availability and reduces central bank policy rigidity, potentially improving access to formal credit for TN-based MSMEs and agriculture.

second_order_effect: Once operational, TN bank can offer state-tailored lending products; however, competitive parity with 27+ existing banks and RBI reserve/CRR requirements limit interest-rate differentiation and actual lending advantage vs. existing SBI/RBI channels.

impact_mechanism: Tamilnad Mercantile Bank (TMB) integration with proposed Tamil National Bank theoretically consolidates TN financial capacity; actual mechanism depends on ownership/governance clarity and capital expansion post-merger.

second_order_effect: Post-merger entity may improve operational efficiency and branch consolidation, but historical TMB credit deployment (~₹5,000 cr outstanding) is limited; scale-up requires significant capital infusion (₹2,000+ cr), competing with other state priorities.

impact_mechanism: Subsidized credit terms (lower interest/extended tenor) reduce debt-service burden for agriculture, dairy, fisheries, SMBs, and self-employed; mechanism is PRICE SUBSIDY on borrowing cost.

third_order_effect: If coupled with extension services and market linkages (not stated), productivity gains compound; without these, subsidy becomes recurring fiscal drain (~₹500-800 cr annually if scaled), crowding out other budgets.

third_order_effect: Agricultural incomes stabilize for credit-eligible farmers; rural consumption and labor-wage demand increase; but widening gap emerges between credit-eligible (landed) and landless agricultural workers, amplifying inequality.

impact_mechanism: Solar capacity addition (5,700 MW) reduces grid dependence on fossil fuels and operational power costs; mechanism is INFRASTRUCTURE PROVISION (renewable energy supply) to lower long-term electricity procurement spend.
first_order_effect: 5,700 MW operational capacity (if achieved by year 5, realistic pace ~800-1,000 MW/yr) displaces ~15-18 TWh annual fossil fuel generation; reduces TN's power procurement cost by ~₹6,000-7,000 cr/yr at scale (₹3.5-4/kWh solar vs ₹6-7/kWh coal).
second_order_effect: Lower aggregate power cost reduces electricity tariff pressure on consumers (esp. agriculture/MSME sectors); industrial competitiveness improves; government fiscal space expands if surplus redirected to other budgets; but jobs in coal/thermal sector face displacement (~2,000-5,000 jobs).
third_order_effect: Long-term: renewable energy attracts green manufacturing FDI (EV, solar equipment); TN positioning as green hub strengthens; however, grid stability risk emerges without simultaneous battery storage/demand management investment (~₹4,000-5,000 cr additional).

impact_mechanism: Rooftop solar on public buildings (schools, hospitals, gov offices) reduces their operational power costs and establishes grid-tied generation reserves; mechanism is INFRASTRUCTURE PROVISION + COST REDUCTION for public institutions.
first_order_effect: 550-1,100 units (assume 100-500 kW each = 55-550 MW total capacity); estimated cost ₹400-600 cr; ~3,500-5,000 public buildings electrified off-grid; annual operational savings ~₹200-300 cr across institutions; typical payback period 5-7 years.
second_order_effect: Reduced operational costs allow schools/hospitals to redirect budgets to teaching/clinical staff and supplies; improved facility uptime esp. in rural hospitals (lower load-shedding risk); buildings become community solar hubs if excess capacity shared via microgrids.
third_order_effect: If microgrids deployed, last-mile electrification of neighboring villages accelerates; rural healthcare quality improves via reliable power; but implementation requires regulatory clarity on net-metering and revenue-sharing (not yet stated), limiting third-order scaling.

impact_mechanism: 24/7 renewable energy supply pledge assumes grid-integrated battery storage or demand-side management; mechanism is ENERGY ACCESS CONTINUITY to eliminate load-shedding and grid downtime.
first_order_effect: Promise is aspirational/not technically feasible at current scale: India's renewable capacity factor ~20-25% (solar) and 35-40% (wind); 24/7 supply requires 4-5x overbuild + storage capacity (~20-30 GWh batteries, cost ₹60,000-80,000 cr), exceeding stated ₹3,500 cr solar budget by 15-20x.
second_order_effect: If interpreted as 'improved baseload via storage,' realistic outcome by 2029: 3-4 GWh battery capacity enables 2-3 hours of load-shedding elimination for critical sectors only; agricultural/domestic consumers still face intermittency; political credibility damage if promise unstated as infeasible.

second_order_effect: Lower wholesale electricity prices reduce transmission losses and baseline tariff for all connected consumers; industrial competitiveness improves, attracting manufacturing investment to TN.
third_order_effect: Sustained industrial growth increases tax revenue and employment, offsetting capex burden; TN positions as renewable-energy-led manufacturing hub, creating 50,000+ jobs in supply chain and operations over 5 years.

promise_text_en: Provide 24-hour uninterrupted power supply to agricultural and industrial sectors
impact_mechanism: Guaranteed 24-hour uninterrupted supply eliminates agricultural pump cutoffs and industrial production downtime, removing the power-scarcity constraint on farm productivity and manufacturing output.
first_order_effect: Agricultural and industrial sectors receive 24/7 grid supply; assumes ~2.5 crore farm households + ~50,000 registered factories benefit; eliminates rationing/rotational cuts affecting these sectors.
second_order_effect: Consistent power enables 2-3 crop cycles/year instead of 1-2, increases irrigation-dependent cropping area, and permits 24-hour shift-work in industries, raising output by estimated 15-25% in affected sectors.
third_order_effect: Higher sectoral productivity drives rural income growth, industrial competitiveness, and state GSDP expansion; creates fiscal space via increased GST/electricity revenue to fund other commitments.

third_order_effect: Sustained renewable capacity growth reduces thermal generation share, lowering long-term fuel costs and supporting TN's net-zero pathway while decreasing energy-cost competitiveness gap vs. other states.

third_order_effect: Lower peaking power costs reduce average electricity tariffs; fossil fuel import dependence declines; energy security improves and carbon emissions trajectory accelerates toward net-zero.

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure provision for fishing supply chain reduces post-harvest losses and enables higher-value export markets through cold storage and processing capacity.
first_order_effect: 3 new ports, 6 fishing harbours, 8 repair facilities, 4 fish landing centres with storage, 4 ice factories, 5 feed plants, and 16 processing units become operational; estimated direct employment ~12,000–15,000 workers across construction, operations, and allied services.

third_order_effect: Formalized fishing supply chains integrate into state export corridors, driving upstream demand for mechanized vessels and feed, and downstream demand for branded packaged fish products; fisher incomes stabilize and diversify beyond seasonal catch volatility.

third_order_effect: Improved credit access lowers agricultural volatility and enables investment in mechanization or land improvement; however, without crop insurance or price support, commodity-price shocks still trigger default cycles and asset distress sales.

impact_mechanism: Crop insurance provides income-shock protection, enabling farmers to continue consumption and reinvestment after crop failure or price collapse.
first_order_effect: Crop insurance schemes (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana, Weather-based Index Insurance, or state-specific schemes) extended or supplemented; estimated 20–30 lakh farmer households enrolled; payout triggers on documented yield loss or weather event.
second_order_effect: Indemnified farmers experience lower income volatility after crop failure; debt default and distress asset sales reduce; farm households maintain consumption and school attendance of children during loss years.
third_order_effect: Reduced income risk enables farmer investment in higher-value crops and soil health; however, scheme payouts often lag implementation (claims take 6–12 months), and exclusion of smallholders (unregistered land, informal tenancy) limits reach to ~50–60% of vulnerable farmers.

promise_text_en: Agricultural infrastructure including irrigation systems, storage facilities, and market linkages will be developed
promise_text_ta: Mö£‚è£ôƒèO™ îƒ°‹ M´Fèœ
impact_mechanism: Agricultural infrastructure (irrigation, storage, market linkage) addresses productivity and marketing bottlenecks, raising farm output and farmgate prices through reduced spoilage and improved price discovery.
first_order_effect: Irrigation systems expand or upgrade (estimated 2–5 lakh hectares), farm storage facilities constructed, and market linkages (e-NAM integration, direct procurement, cold chains) operationalized; productivity increase ~15–25% on irrigated land; post-harvest losses decline from ~25–30% to ~10–15%.
second_order_effect: Higher output and reduced losses raise gross farm revenue by 20–30%; farmers reduce debt-driven input purchases and shift to retained earnings for reinvestment; farmgate prices improve due to reduced information asymmetry and bulk marketing.
third_order_effect: Sustained higher farm incomes enable long-term investment in soil health, crop diversification, and human capital (children's education); farmer exit to non-farm livelihoods slows; rural consumption and secondary-sector growth accelerate.

promise_text_en: Support for agricultural equipment, seeds, and input supplies for farmers
promise_text_ta: ÝJó‚èí‚è£ù «è£J™èœ, º‚Aò ñÅFèœ, î˜è£‚èœ
impact_mechanism: Input subsidy reduces the per-unit cost of production for farmers, lowering the capital barrier to cultivation and enabling higher-acreage operations.
first_order_effect: Farmers gain access to subsidized equipment, seeds, and supplies; estimated reach ~8-12 lakh marginal/small farmers in TN, reducing input costs by 20-30% per season.
second_order_effect: Lower input costs increase net farm income in the short term, but without demand-side guarantees or value-chain integration, farmers may face margin compression if output prices remain stagnant or decline due to oversupply.
third_order_effect: Over 3-5 years, supply-side subsidy alone without market linkages may entrench input-dependent cultivation patterns and postpone structural shift toward high-value crops or farm consolidation.

promise_text_en: Agricultural extension services and technical support for modern farming practices
promise_text_ta: ªî£™Lò™ ²ŸÁô£
impact_mechanism: Technical advisory and extension services transfer knowledge of yield-enhancing and climate-adaptive practices directly to farmers, increasing productive efficiency without additional capital outlay.
first_order_effect: Trained farmers adopt drip irrigation, soil testing, and improved crop varieties; estimated 5-8 lakh farmers engaged through block-level extension staff, with potential 15-25% yield gains in adopter cohorts.
second_order_effect: Higher yields increase gross farm income; adoption clusters create peer-learning networks that reduce information asymmetry and accelerate diffusion of practices across villages.
third_order_effect: Sustained knowledge transfer over 5+ years shifts farming from input-responsive to knowledge-responsive, reducing dependence on government input subsidies and enabling farmers to respond independently to price and climate signals.

promise_text_en: Livestock support and animal husbandry development programs

impact_mechanism: Livestock support programs (breeding, veterinary care, feed subsidies) diversify farm household income and de-risk agricultural income by creating a parallel revenue stream from dairy, poultry, or meat production.
first_order_effect: Estimated 15-20 lakh rural households adopt or intensify livestock rearing; monthly income per household increases by ₹2,000-4,000 from milk/egg sales, reducing reliance on seasonal crop income.
second_order_effect: Diversified income reduces vulnerability to crop failure; households can invest surplus into land improvement or education, and women gain control over livestock income (high female participation in dairying).
third_order_effect: Livestock integration stabilizes rural livelihoods and reduces distress migration; improved animal nutrition and breeding over time builds community-level animal husbandry capacity and creates local fodder value chains.

promise_text_en: Rural water supply and irrigation infrastructure development

impact_mechanism: Water infrastructure expansion increases irrigable area and reduces groundwater depletion, enabling year-round cultivation and diversification into water-intensive crops.
first_order_effect: Estimated 20-30 lakh hectares brought under reliable irrigation (from minor works, groundwater recharge, and pipeline networks); cropping intensity increases from ~1.8x to ~2.2x in beneficiary taluks.
second_order_effect: Expanded irrigation enables shift from rainfed to irrigated high-value crops (sugarcane, coconut, spices), increasing farm household incomes by 40-60%; however, water-intensive monoculture may accelerate depletion if extraction governance is weak.
third_order_effect: Long-term sustainability depends on embedded water management (aquifer monitoring, farmer collectives for demand-side rationing); without this, infrastructure gain may be offset by renewed scarcity within 5-10 years.

promise_text_en: Establish journalists' welfare scheme with monthly assistance of ₹12,000 to ₹15,000

impact_mechanism: Direct cash transfer (₹12,000-15,000/month) provides income floor for journalists, reducing economic precarity and dependence on patronage or advertorial income that compromises editorial independence.
first_order_effect: Approximately 8,000-15,000 registered journalists eligible; annual fiscal cost ~₹115-270 cr; immediate income security for beneficiaries, reducing financial desperation.
second_order_effect: Journalists with stable income are better positioned to resist advertorial pressure and invest time in investigative reporting; editorial quality may improve in beneficiary newsrooms, but the scheme does not mandate accountability or prevent continuation of unethical practices.
third_order_effect: If scheme is conditional on professional codes of conduct (transparency, conflict-of-interest disclosure), it can strengthen media accountability ecosystems; otherwise it is income support without editorial reform.

impact_mechanism: Accreditation cards formalize journalist status and create a verifiable credential for professional recognition, reducing gatekeeping power of media houses and improving access to institutional sources.
first_order_effect: Journalists holding accreditation cards gain institutional access (police, courts, government offices) and professional legitimacy; estimated 10,000-20,000 card-holders, improving their reporting capacity and status.
second_order_effect: Formalized credentials reduce dependence on media house affiliation for source access, enabling independent/freelance journalists to report; however, if enforcement is lax, cards may be issued without professional vetting, diluting credibility.
third_order_effect: Over time, accreditation can anchor a professionalization ecosystem (continuing education, ethics boards, dispute resolution), but only if coupled with regular credential renewal and conduct-based suspension provisions.

impact_mechanism: Infrastructure transfer (cold chain, chillers, milk collection centers) to 30,000 SHGs reduces post-harvest losses and transaction costs, enabling higher-margin milk marketing and collective bargaining power.
first_order_effect: 30,000 SHGs (approximately 3-4 lakh women members) gain access to modern milk handling infrastructure; milk loss reduces from 15-20% to <5%; members earn 25-35% higher prices via chilled milk vs. fresh sale.
second_order_effect: Higher milk prices increase SHG member income; collective milk pools reduce individual transaction costs and increase bargaining power against middlemen; SHGs transition from informal to semi-formal dairy enterprises.
third_order_effect: Strengthened SHGs attract external credit and technical services; dairy value chains formalize; cooperative capacity for collective input purchase and extension services develops, reducing dependence on government subsidies.

third_order_effect: Without complementary fodder production infrastructure and veterinary capacity, jersey adoption may increase input costs and debt burden, offsetting milk price gains; sustainability depends on coupling breed support with fodder security and animal health services.

impact_mechanism: Skill building in high-value livestock activities enables farmers to diversify income streams and increase per-animal productivity through improved husbandry practices.

third_order_effect: Sustained income growth enables debt repayment, increased reinvestment in livestock assets, and gradual transition from subsistence to commercial dairy farming; cooperative membership strengthens as members perceive tangible returns.

impact_mechanism: Expanding court capacity directly reduces judicial queue length, enabling faster case resolution and reducing litigation cost burden on litigants.
first_order_effect: 50 new courts operationalized within 10 months; case-to-judge ratio improves from ~500-600 to ~450-550 cases per judge in beneficiary districts; case pendency decreases by ~8-12% in TN judicial system.
second_order_effect: Faster judgment timelines reduce litigant spending on repeated appearances and legal fees; economically disadvantaged parties (esp. land disputes, debt recovery) experience reduced financial strain during litigation.
third_order_effect: Lower litigation costs increase poor households' willingness to pursue legitimate claims (property, succession, tenant rights); property rights enforcement strengthens in rural areas; informal resolution mechanisms decline as formal courts become accessible.

impact_mechanism: Adequate court infrastructure (chambers, waiting areas, technology) reduces judge fatigue and administrative delays, improving case disposition rates and litigant experience.
first_order_effect: Court facilities upgraded in new and existing courts; judges and staff have functional working environments; technology integration (digital filing, video conferencing capability) enabled in ~50-100 courts.
second_order_effect: Reduced procedural delays and improved case scheduling efficiency; litigant appearance rates improve as courts become easier to access and navigate; judges can maintain higher case output per sitting.
third_order_effect: System-wide case clearance rate improves by 5-8%; reduced backlogs create psychological shift from 'courts are blocked' to functional grievance redressal; trust in judicial system increases in covered districts.

impact_mechanism: Hybrid proceedings (in-person + virtual) eliminate geographic and transportation barriers, enabling litigants in remote areas to participate without traveling to court towns.
first_order_effect: ~30-50% of non-critical proceedings (arguments, document submissions, status hearings) conducted via video conferencing in pilot courts; litigants in rural taluks avoid 2-3 day travel per court appearance.
second_order_effect: Transportation and opportunity costs fall by 60-70% per court visit; economically weaker litigants can attend more hearings without income loss; case participation improves, reducing default judgments.
third_order_effect: Rural-urban litigation equity improves; formal court access substitutes for informal dispute resolution in remote areas; women litigants (esp. those with mobility constraints) increase engagement with formal justice system.

promise_text_en: Establish Judicial Accountability & Research Committee to monitor court performance and conduct research on judicial matters

impact_mechanism: Institutional monitoring of court performance creates feedback loops for case management improvement, enabling data-driven identification of bottlenecks and judge productivity gaps.
first_order_effect: Committee established; baseline performance data collected on case disposal rates, average case duration, judge caseload, and adjournment patterns across TN courts; quarterly performance reports published.
second_order_effect: Performance transparency enables administrative action (judge reallocation, case reassignment, adjournment reduction); courts identified as under-performing receive targeted interventions; incentive structures shift toward case clearance.
third_order_effect: Systemic accountability embeds case management culture; high-performing judges become exemplars; case disposal rates improve gradually 3-5%; litigant trust in judicial impartiality increases due to visible performance standards.

promise_text_en: Establish Post-Conviction Exoneration Commission or Innocence Commission to review wrongful convictions

impact_mechanism: Institutional review of wrongful convictions provides legal remedy and compensation for falsely imprisoned individuals, addressing a structural failure of conviction accuracy.
first_order_effect: Commission established; case review process operationalized; ~50-200 convictions reviewed in first 2 years (TN averages ~10-20 exonerations annually); identified wrongful convictions vacated; compensation disbursed (~₹5-15 lakh per case).
second_order_effect: Exonerated individuals receive financial restitution and legal clearing; systemic review identifies weak evidence practices, witness reliability issues, and procedural failures; police/prosecution practices scrutinized and corrected.
third_order_effect: Criminal justice standards improve (stricter evidence rules, witness corroboration requirements); wrongful conviction rate declines gradually; public confidence in conviction legitimacy increases; compensation payouts create fiscal pressure incentivizing quality control in investigation/prosecution.

promise_text_en: Enact new criminal law codes replacing Indian Penal Code, Criminal Procedure Code, and Indian Evidence Act with updated versions

scheme_name: Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita / Bhartiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita / Bhartiya Sakshya Adhiniyam
impact_mechanism: Modernized criminal codes replace 160+ year-old statutes with contemporary offense categories, penalty structures, and procedural safeguards aligned with current crime patterns and constitutional standards.
first_order_effect: New criminal codes enacted; ~50-100 archaic/obsolete offenses removed; sentencing frameworks aligned to offense severity; procedural protections (police custody limits, bail presumptions) strengthened; implementation phased over 2-3 years.
second_order_effect: Litigation timelines shorten due to clearer offense definitions and streamlined procedures; frivolous prosecutions decline due to stricter thresholds; judicial discretion narrows, reducing sentencing disparity; accused persons benefit from bail and custody safeguards.
third_order_effect: Criminal justice becomes more predictable and equitable; wrongful prosecution risk declines; prison overcrowding eases due to bail-friendliness and reduced sentence lengths; long-term crime deterrence effects depend on enforcement consistency, not code structure.

second_order_effect: Lower input wastage and improved germination rates increase farm yields by 10-15%, raising per-hectare income for small/marginal farmers by ₹3,000-5,000 annually.
third_order_effect: Sustained yield gains reduce farmer debt cycles and create surplus for input investment, gradually shifting cultivation from subsistence to market-oriented farming in seed-dependent crops.

impact_mechanism: Strengthened seed certification standards enforce rigorous germination and purity testing, reducing genetic/physiological seed defects that cause crop failure.

second_order_effect: Reliable seed availability increases farmer confidence in purchasing certified varieties, shifting market share from loose/uncertified to certified seeds; seed companies invest in quality infrastructure.
third_order_effect: Improved seed quality becomes baseline expectation, incentivizing formal seed sector consolidation and driving out unregulated traders; agricultural productivity gains compound over 5-year crop cycles.

first_order_effect: Coastal areas (~1,076 km shoreline in TN) zoned into no-development, restricted, and permissible zones; ~15-20% of currently vulnerable coastal land protected from industrial/real estate encroachment within 3 years.
second_order_effect: Protected fishing grounds maintain fish populations; reduced saltwater intrusion into agricultural land; mangrove/wetland preservation sustains biodiversity and coastal livelihoods for ~2.5 lakh fisher households.
third_order_effect: Long-term marine ecosystem stability supports sustainable fishery yields and climate adaptation (storm surge buffering); coastal communities' income bases become less vulnerable to environmental collapse.

second_order_effect: Accelerated afforestation increases forest cover by 3-5%, improving water retention and reducing soil erosion; forest-dependent communities (tribal/NTFP collectors) gain improved access to sustainably managed forest resources.
third_order_effect: Enhanced carbon sequestration and groundwater recharge reduce long-term climate vulnerability; increased biomass availability supports fuel, fodder, and livelihood diversification for ~1.5 lakh forest-adjacent households.

promise_text_en: Enact Fisherwomen Protection and Empowerment Bill 2025 to ensure fisherwomen are recognised as fishery workers with collective bargaining rights and improved workplace protections.

impact_mechanism: Legal recognition and collective bargaining rights enable fisherwomen to negotiate wages, working conditions, and access to marine resources on equal terms with male workers.

second_order_effect: Wage premiums of 15-25% realized for recognized work; access to social security schemes (ESIC, maternity benefits) reduces household income volatility; fisherwomen's economic autonomy increases household bargaining power.
third_order_effect: Improved household income stability increases girls' school enrollment in coastal communities by 10-15%; fisherwomen's voices in resource management reduce overfishing and support sustainable marine practices.

promise_text_en: Enact Labour Laws Bill to recognise domestic workers as formal workers, establish workplace protections, ensure access to social security, and guarantee collective bargaining rights.

first_order_effect: ~12-15 lakh domestic workers (80% women) registered as formal workers; workplace protection standards enforced; access to ESIC, PF, and accident insurance becomes mandatory; estimated wage floor increase of 20-30%.
second_order_effect: Reduced turnover and improved retention strengthen household-worker relationships; workers invest in skill development and safe working practices; employers face compliance costs but benefit from stable workforce and reduced legal liability.
third_order_effect: Formalized domestic work sector becomes a stable, replicable income source; domestic workers' children gain educational stability; pathway created for sectoral upgrades (certification, cooperative formation, career progression).

impact_mechanism: Mega port infrastructure attracts containerized cargo traffic and maritime logistics employment, creating export-oriented jobs and reducing transport costs for manufacturers across TN.
first_order_effect: Port capacity expansion in Chennai-Tirunelveli corridor; estimated 15,000–25,000 direct construction and port operations jobs; potential ₹2,500–4,000 cr annual port revenue by Year 5.
second_order_effect: Lower logistics costs for exporters trigger manufacturing consolidation in TN; competing ports (Cochin, Visakhapatnam) pressure TN to invest further in terminal capacity and dredging, creating a virtuous cycle of infrastructure investment.
third_order_effect: Regional shift in manufacturing clusters toward coastal zones; potential displacement of inland micro-industries; long-term environmental stress (dredging, coastal erosion, ecosystem disruption) offsetting economic gains.

promise_text_en: Develop new thermal power generation capacity through NLC Mining sectors (Mine II and Mine III) with ₹10,000 crore investment
promise_text_ta: ªïŒ«õLJ™ îŸ«ð£¶œ÷ Þó‡ì£õ¶ Gô‚èK„ ²óƒèˆ¬î (Mine II) MK¾ð´ˆ¶õ¶ì¡, ¹Fî£è Í¡ø£õ¶ Gô‚èK„ ²óƒèˆ¬î (Mine III) Ü¬ñ‚è¾‹ `10,000 «è£®‚°‹ «ñô£ù ñFŠd†®™ F†ìIìŠð†´œ÷¶
impact_mechanism: Thermal power generation capacity addresses electricity supply constraints, enabling industrial growth and reducing power tariffs through increased baseload supply.
first_order_effect: NLC Mine II and III expansion generates ~2,000–3,500 MW new capacity; ~8,000–12,000 direct mining and power plant jobs; ₹10,000 cr capex spread over 4–5 years strains TN discretionary budget (₹184,162 cr available).
second_order_effect: Increased coal-based power supply reduces per-unit tariffs by 5–8%, benefiting large industrial consumers and urban households; however, peak-hour demand gaps persist due to coal supply volatility and grid integration delays.
third_order_effect: Lower industrial tariffs attract energy-intensive manufacturing (cement, steel, chemicals); carbon-intensive growth locks TN into coal dependency; renewable energy targets (MNRE commitments) compete for investment, delaying decarbonization.

promise_text_en: Establish petrochemical and oil refineries using IOCL, ONGC, and other public and private sector projects to boost manufacturing in Tamil Nadu
promise_text_ta: ªèJ™ âKõ£»‚ °ö£ŒŠ ðFŠ¹ˆ F†ì‹, IOCL-¡ °ö£ŒŠ ðF‚°‹ F†ì‹, ONGC-¡ âKõ£»‚ °ö£ŒŠ ðF‚°‹ F†ì‹, ÞòŸ¬è âKè£ŸÁ ñŸÁ‹ cóè‚èKñ‹
impact_mechanism: Petrochemical and refinery infrastructure diversifies manufacturing base away from textiles and auto, creating high-value processing employment and downstream chemical industries.
first_order_effect: IOCL/ONGC refinery and petrochemical complex approval and land acquisition; estimated 5,000–8,000 direct jobs (construction + operations); ₹8,000–15,000 cr private/public capex; 3–4 year construction-to-production timeline.
second_order_effect: Established refinery reduces feedstock costs for downstream chemical manufacturers; attracts polymer, paint, and specialty chemical firms; however, air and water pollution in refinery zones (Thoothukudi, South Arcot model) triggers health complaints and contingent liabilities.
third_order_effect: Manufacturing consolidation in refinery-adjacent special economic zones; long-term environmental remediation costs (~₹500–800 cr per zone) create fiscal drag; worker health claims accumulate, offsetting tax revenue gains.

promise_text_en: Develop Maritime sector infrastructure with focus on coastal regulation zones and environmental protection in coastal districts
promise_text_ta: èì«ô£ó å¿ƒ°º¬ø ñ‡ìô (CRZ) MFèO™ î÷˜¾èœ ãŸð´ˆîŠð´A¡øù. Þ¶ eùõ˜èO¡ õ¬ô H¡Â‹ Þìƒèœ, õ£›Mìƒèœ ñŸÁ‹ ðì° GÁˆ¶Iìƒè¬÷ ¬èòèŠð´ˆî õNõ°‚Aø¶
impact_mechanism: Maritime infrastructure development and coastal regulation compliance reduce permitting delays and environmental liability, enabling sustainable port operations and fisheries co-management.
first_order_effect: Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) mapping and environmental impact assessments completed for ~1,200 km coastline; ~50–80 new maritime projects approved; estimated 3,000–5,000 jobs in coastal tourism, port services, and marine conservation.
second_order_effect: Faster project approvals (6–12 months vs. 18–24 months historical) encourage private investment in ports, shipyards, and fish-processing units; however, weak enforcement of CRZ norms (historical pattern: Ennore, Thoothukudi) enables illegal construction.
third_order_effect: Ecosystem degradation (mangrove loss, fish stock collapse) compounds over 5–10 years; fisher community income declines offset by maritime sector growth; climate vulnerability of coastal infrastructure increases due to deferred mangrove restoration.

promise_text_en: Develop Renewable Energy in power sector including coal-based and non-coal-based generation capacity with focus on employment creation
promise_text_ta: M¬÷„êL™ Þ¼‰î ªï™ ðJ˜è¬÷Š ªð¼‹ Þò‚Aèœ ªè£‡´ ÜNˆ¶ Gôƒèœ ¬èòèŠð´ˆîŠð†ìù
impact_mechanism: Renewable energy capacity addition reduces fossil-fuel dependence and creates distributed employment in solar panel installation, wind turbine operations, and grid management.
first_order_effect: Estimated 10,000–15,000 MW solar + wind capacity added over 5 years (TN is already at ~15,000 MW RE); ~20,000–30,000 jobs in manufacturing, installation, O&M; ₹3,000–5,000 cr annual investment required.
second_order_effect: Increased RE penetration (from current ~28% to 35–40%) lowers average generation cost (LCOE of solar: ₹2.5–3.5/kWh vs. coal: ₹4.5–5.5/kWh); however, grid intermittency challenges persist, requiring ₹500–800 cr battery storage infrastructure.
third_order_effect: Long-term: coal plant utilization drops, stranding assets in existing thermal fleet; worker retrenchment in coal mining (~1,000–2,000 jobs lost); solar manufacturing consolidation in TN creates competitive advantage, but import competition (Chinese cells) limits value capture.

promise_text_en: Support National Thermal Power Corporation (NTPC) coal mines and develop 2,600 hectares for new industries and 1,300 hectares for essential sectors (agriculture, dairy, animal husbandry)

impact_mechanism: Land allocation for agriculture, dairy, and animal husbandry sectors supports small-holder farming and rural livelihoods through direct land provision and enabling cooperative infrastructure.
first_order_effect: 1,300 hectares allocated to agriculture/dairy/animal husbandry (~100–150 clusters of 8–10 hectares each); estimated 15,000–20,000 small farmers and dairy producers gain access to organized production zones; 2,600 hectares for industrial parks attract ~400–600 MSMEs.
second_order_effect: Organized land parcels reduce fragmentation-induced productivity loss; cooperative dairy units (500–1,000-liter capacity per cluster) enable bulk milk collection, processing, and market linkage; average farm income increases 15–25%; however, water scarcity in allocated zones (Tiruppur, Madurai regions) limits yield gains.
third_order_effect: Agricultural productivity gains reduce rural poverty headcount by 2–4 percentage points (~20–40 lakh people) over 5–7 years; increased agri-waste (post-harvest residue) creates secondary compost/biogas industries; organized dairying reduces informal market exploitation but increases input costs (feed, veterinary).

promise_text_en: Allocate ₹27,400 crore for NTPC Neyveli expansion with 5,746 MW thermal power generation capacity
promise_text_ta: è£…C¹ó‹ ñ£õ†ì‹ ðó‰ÉK™ `27,400 «è£® ñFŠd†®™ ªê¡¬ù‚è£ù Þó‡ì£õ¶ õ£Û˜F G¬ôò‹ Ü¬ñ‚è å¡Pò ñŸÁ‹ ñ£Gô Üó²èœ F†ìI†´œ÷ù. êŸªø£Šð 5,746 °Á‚è‹ ðóŠð÷M™ ÞˆF†ì‹ º¡ªñ£NòŠð†´œ÷¶
impact_mechanism: Thermal power capacity expansion via NTPC Neyveli supplies baseload electricity to industrial zones and households, reducing power tariffs and enabling continuous manufacturing operations.
first_order_effect: 5,746 MW capacity added over 4–5 years at Neyveli complex; estimated 12,000–18,000 construction jobs + 2,500–3,500 permanent operational staff; ₹27,400 cr capex represents 15% of TN's annual discretionary budget, requiring debt/central funding.
second_order_effect: Increased coal-based baseload reduces peak tariff surcharges; industries (cement, steel, fertilizer) reduce captive power investment and shift to grid supply; peak-hour shortage margin narrows from current 8–12% to 3–5%, improving industrial reliability.
third_order_effect: Manufacturing expansion in TN accelerates; however, coal ash disposal (estimated 15–20 lakh tonnes/year at full capacity) requires ₹400–600 cr land and environmental remediation investment; long-term stranding of thermal assets post-2035 (India's 2070 net-zero target) erodes residual value.

promise_text_en: Complete 8 new thermal power plants (cooling towers) for thermal power generation, with establishment of new power stations in all districts and supply of clean power across Tamil Nadu
promise_text_ta: «ñŸ°ˆ ªî£ì˜„C ñ¬ôŠð°FJ™ 1300 ñ£ˆFK ÝöˆF™ GÁõŠð´‹ Þ‰î ÝŒ¾‚Ãì‹ Ü¬ñŠðîŸ° 8 Þô‚è‹ ÜJó„Yªó¬ì â¬ì»œ÷ Üè›Mìƒè¬÷ (°õ£Kè¬÷) ªõ®¬õˆ¶
impact_mechanism: Distributed thermal power plants across districts improve rural electrification reliability and enable decentralized industrial clusters, reducing transmission losses and peak-demand spikes.
first_order_effect: 8 new cooling tower units + district-level power stations add 1,300 MW net capacity; estimated 8,000–12,000 construction jobs + 1,500–2,000 O&M staff across districts; estimated cost ₹5,500–7,000 cr (not explicitly budgeted).
second_order_effect: Distributed generation reduces transmission losses (from current ~6–8% to 4–6%); rural electrification improves from current 96% household access to 98%+ reliable supply; however, coal evacuation bottlenecks (railway capacity) and water scarcity for cooling limit operational efficiency.
third_order_effect: Rural manufacturing clusters (agro-processing, textiles) expand with reliable power; agricultural pump operations stabilize, reducing seasonal groundwater depletion stress by 5–10%; long-term: coal-based model becomes stranded asset, requiring ₹1,000+ cr write-down post-2035.

promise_text_en: Establish Green Copper Plant and modernize industrial facilities with soil restoration initiatives

impact_mechanism: Establishes manufacturing capacity and soil remediation infrastructure, creating local industrial employment and reversing agronomic degradation in affected regions.
first_order_effect: Green Copper Plant operationalized with soil restoration work initiated in mining/industrial zones; direct employment for plant operations and soil survey/remediation teams (~500-2,000 jobs estimated).
second_order_effect: Improved soil fertility in restored areas increases agricultural productivity and crop yields for smallholder farmers on reclaimed land; reduced input costs from healthier soil.
third_order_effect: Sustained income growth for farming households in remediation zones reduces rural distress migration; plant revenue creates state tax base to fund rural infrastructure.

promise_text_en: Establish up to 10,000 renewable energy units in villages, with priority to solar and wind energy projects

impact_mechanism: Decentralized energy production access via solar/wind units reduces grid dependence and energy costs for rural households and agricultural operations.
first_order_effect: 10,000 renewable energy units installed across villages; estimated 5,000-7,000 households + agricultural pumps gain direct access to low-cost electricity; cumulative installed capacity ~50-100 MW (depending on unit scale).
second_order_effect: Reduced energy bills enable farmers to operate pump sets longer during irrigation season; improved water access boosts crop productivity; marginal income savings redirect to health/education spending.
third_order_effect: Rural electrification triggers ancillary enterprise development (cold storage, agro-processing); improved water security stabilizes agricultural income and reduces debt-driven distress sales.

promise_text_en: Complete Units 1 and 2 of Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant (KKNPP) and advance Units 3, 4, 5, and 6 to be completed by 2026

impact_mechanism: Nuclear capacity expansion supplies baseload electricity to power industrial growth, reduce coal dependence, and lower long-term generation costs for large-scale consumption.
first_order_effect: KKNPP Units 1 & 2 commissioned (~2,000 MW); Units 3–6 advanced toward 2026 completion (target additional ~4,000 MW); immediate grid stabilization and ~1,500–2,500 construction/operations jobs; central government responsibility for timeline.
second_order_effect: Increased reliable baseload supply reduces power cuts for industry; lower wholesale electricity cost trajectory supports manufacturing competitiveness and attracts industrial investment to TN.
third_order_effect: Industrial clustering and export-oriented growth creates secondary employment in manufacturing, logistics, and services; state GST revenue grows; carbon intensity of grid declines.

promise_text_en: Implement renewable energy initiatives aligned with COP28 climate targets to maintain 1.5°C temperature limit

impact_mechanism: Scaling renewable energy capacity reduces thermal power generation share, cutting state-level carbon emissions and aligning TN's energy mix with global climate commitments.
first_order_effect: Renewable energy projects initiated/expanded (solar farms, wind installations, rooftop solar incentives); cumulative capacity additions target ~2,000–3,000 MW; emissions avoidance ~2–3 million tonnes CO₂ over 5-year term.
second_order_effect: Lower thermal coal procurement reduces air pollution health costs; state builds green energy credential attracting ESG-linked climate finance and green bonds for infrastructure funding.
third_order_effect: Sustained renewable investment reduces long-term energy cost trajectory; enables TN to meet net-zero targets by 2070 (aligned with national NDC); positions state as clean-tech hub.
